verses.explainer: Thus the people are divided because of Jesus. Division is the consistent response to Jesus throughout John's Gospel. He is never met with indifference.

verses.text: Some of them would have arrested him, but no one laid hands on him.
verses.explainer: Some want to seize him, but no one lays a hand on him. Again, the divine timing holds. Human hostility cannot override God's plan.

verses.text: The officers therefore came to the chief priests and Pharisees, and they said to them, “Why didn’t you bring him?”
verses.explainer: The Temple guards return to the chief priests and Pharisees empty-handed. The authorities demand: 'Why didn't you bring him in?'

verses.text: The officers answered, “No man ever spoke like this man!”
verses.explainer: The guards answer: 'No one ever spoke the way this man does.' Even the police are disarmed by Jesus' words. They were sent to arrest him and came back as involuntary witnesses.

verses.text: The Pharisees therefore answered them, “You aren’t also led astray, are you?
verses.explainer: The Pharisees retort: 'Are you deceived too?' They are frustrated that even their own officers are falling under Jesus' influence.

verses.text: Have any of the rulers believed in him, or of the Pharisees?
verses.explainer: Have any of the rulers or Pharisees believed in him? The argument from authority: if the experts reject him, the common people should too.

verses.text: But this multitude that doesn’t know the law is accursed.”
verses.explainer: This mob that knows nothing of the law -- there is a curse on them. The Pharisees dismiss the crowd as ignorant. Their contempt for ordinary people is barely concealed.

verses.text: Nicodemus (he who came to him by night, being one of them) said to them,
verses.explainer: Nicodemus, who had gone to Jesus earlier and who was one of their own, speaks up.

verses.text: “Does our law judge a man, unless it first hears from him personally and knows what he does?”
verses.explainer: Nicodemus asks: 'Does our law condemn a man without first hearing him to find out what he is doing?' He does not defend Jesus directly but appeals to legal procedure. It is a cautious but courageous intervention.

verses.text: They answered him, “Are you also from Galilee? Search, and see that no prophet has arisen out of Galilee.”
verses.explainer: They retort: 'Are you from Galilee too? Look into it, and you will find that a prophet does not come out of Galilee.' Their objection is historically inaccurate -- Jonah was from Gath-hepher in Galilee. But their prejudice against Galilee overrides their knowledge of their own Scriptures.

verses.text: Everyone went to his own house,
verses.explainer: Then they all go home. The council disperses without resolution. The division over Jesus extends into the highest levels of religious authority.

explainer.summary: Jesus declares 'I am the light of the world' during the Feast of Tabernacles, when the Temple was illuminated by giant candelabra. A fierce debate with the Jewish authorities follows, escalating through charges and counter-charges about Abraham, slavery, freedom, and the devil. The chapter climaxes with Jesus' most explosive claim: 'Before Abraham was, I AM' -- a direct appropriation of God's name that provokes an attempt to stone him.
themes: I AM the light of the world (second I AM statement)
Truth and freedom
Children of Abraham vs. children of the devil
Before Abraham was, I AM
The pre-existence of Christ
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter continues the Tabernacles setting from chapter 7. The light ceremony provides the backdrop for Jesus' second 'I AM' statement. The debate with the authorities reaches its most intense point, with Jesus making claims that leave no middle ground -- he is either God or a blasphemer.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The second 'I AM' statement
wordStudies.meaning: The second 'I AM' statement. Spoken during Tabernacles when four great golden candelabra lit up the Temple court. Jesus claims to be what the candelabra symbolized -- the divine light that illuminates all of creation.

wordStudies.translatedAs: In Greek, 'I am
wordStudies.meaning: In Greek, 'I am.' But when Jesus says 'Before Abraham was, I AM,' the present tense is deliberate and absolute. It echoes God's self-revelation in Exodus 3:14 ('I AM WHO I AM'). Jesus claims not just pre-existence but the divine identity itself.

verses.text: but Jesus went to the Mount of Olives.
verses.explainer: Jesus goes to the Mount of Olives. This is his regular retreat location when in Jerusalem, a quiet place across the Kidron Valley from the Temple.

verses.text: Now very early in the morning, he came again into the temple, and all the people came to him. He sat down, and taught them.
verses.explainer: At dawn he appears again in the Temple courts, where all the people gather around him, and he begins to teach. His teaching ministry in the Temple continues.

verses.text: The scribes and the Pharisees brought a woman taken in adultery. Having set her in the middle,
verses.explainer: The teachers of the law and the Pharisees bring in a woman caught in adultery. They make her stand before the group. Note: many scholars believe 8:1-11 (the woman caught in adultery) was not in the original manuscript of John but is an authentic tradition preserved here. It does not appear in the earliest Greek manuscripts.

verses.text: they told him, “Teacher, we found this woman in adultery, in the very act.
verses.explainer: They say to Jesus: 'Teacher, this woman was caught in the act of adultery.' The test is being set up -- they want to force Jesus into a legal dilemma.

verses.text: Now in our law, Moses commanded us to stone such women. What then do you say about her?”
verses.explainer: In the Law Moses commanded us to stone such women. Now what do you say? If Jesus says to stone her, he contradicts his message of grace and risks Roman law (which reserved capital punishment). If he says not to stone her, he contradicts Moses.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Death by stoning for adultery', 'explanation': "Leviticus 20:10 and Deuteronomy 22:22 prescribed death for both parties in adultery. That only the woman was brought raises the question: where is the man? The setup reveals the trap's real purpose -- using this woman as a pawn to test Jesus."}

verses.text:
They said this testing him, that they might have something to accuse him of.
But Jesus stooped down, and wrote on the ground with his finger.
verses.explainer: They are using this question as a trap to have a basis for accusing him. But Jesus bends down and writes on the ground with his finger. What he writes is never revealed -- one of the most tantalizing mysteries in the Gospels.

verses.text:
But when they continued asking him, he looked up and said to them,
“He who is without sin among you, let him throw the first stone at her.”
verses.explainer: When they keep questioning him, Jesus straightens up and says: 'Let any one of you who is without sin be the first to throw a stone at her.' He does not overturn the law; he applies it with devastating precision. The law required witnesses to cast the first stones -- but only if they themselves were qualified.

thenVsNow.then: By requiring sinlessness from the executioners, Jesus did not abolish the penalty but made it impossible to carry out. The law stands, but no one is qualified to enforce it.
thenVsNow.now: This remains one of the most quoted statements attributed to Jesus. It challenges every impulse to condemn others while ignoring one's own failures. It does not eliminate moral standards but insists on humility in applying them.

verses.text: Again he stooped down, and with his finger wrote on the ground.
verses.explainer: Again he stoops down and writes on the ground. The pause gives time for conscience to do its work. Jesus does not look at them as they consider his words.

verses.text: They, when they heard it, being convicted by their conscience, went out one by one, beginning from the oldest, even to the last. Jesus was left alone with the woman where she was, in the middle.
verses.explainer: Those who hear begin to go away one at a time, the older ones first. Experience has taught the older ones how much sin they carry. The woman is left standing alone with Jesus.

verses.text:
Jesus, standing up, saw her and said,
“Woman, where are your accusers? Did no one condemn you?”
verses.explainer: Jesus straightens up and asks: 'Woman, where are they? Has no one condemned you?' He looks for her accusers and finds none.

verses.text:
She said, “No one, Lord.”
Jesus said,
“Neither do I condemn you. Go your way. From now on, sin no more.”
verses.explainer: 'No one, sir,' she says. Jesus declares: 'Then neither do I condemn you. Go now and leave your life of sin.' Grace and moral challenge coexist in one sentence. Jesus does not condone the sin, but he does not condemn the sinner. He offers both forgiveness and a call to transformation.

verses.text:
Again, therefore, Jesus spoke to them, saying,
“I am the light of the world.
He who follows me will not walk in the darkness, but will have the light of life.”
verses.explainer: Jesus speaks to the people again: 'I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will never walk in darkness but will have the light of life.' The second 'I AM' statement, spoken in the Temple court where the great candelabra burned during Tabernacles.

verses.text: The Pharisees therefore said to him, “You testify about yourself. Your testimony is not valid.”
verses.explainer: The Pharisees challenge him: 'You are testifying about yourself; your testimony is not valid.' By Jewish legal standards, self-testimony was insufficient.

verses.text:
Jesus answered them,
“Even if I testify about myself, my testimony is true, for I know where I came from, and where I am going; but you don’t know where I came from, or where I am going.
verses.explainer: Jesus counters: even if he testifies on his own behalf, his testimony is valid because he knows where he came from and where he is going. They do not know his origin or destination.

verses.text: You judge according to the flesh. I judge no one.
verses.explainer: You judge by human standards. I pass judgment on no one. Their judgment is superficial and earthly; Jesus operates on a different plane.

verses.text: Even if I do judge, my judgment is true, for I am not alone, but I am with the Father who sent me.
verses.explainer: But if Jesus does judge, his decisions are true because he is not alone -- the Father who sent him stands with him.

verses.text: It’s also written in your law that the testimony of two people is valid.
verses.explainer: In your own law it is written that the testimony of two witnesses is true. Jesus appeals to their legal standard.

verses.text: I am one who testifies about myself, and the Father who sent me testifies about me.”
verses.explainer: I am one who testifies for myself; my other witness is the Father who sent me. The two required witnesses are Jesus himself and God the Father.

verses.text:
They said therefore to him, “Where is your Father?”
Jesus answered,
“You know neither me, nor my Father. If you knew me, you would know my Father also.”
verses.explainer: They ask: 'Where is your father?' Jesus answers: 'You do not know me or my Father. If you knew me, you would know my Father also.' Knowing Jesus is the way to know God. Rejecting Jesus means rejecting God.

verses.text: Jesus spoke these words in the treasury, as he taught in the temple. Yet no one arrested him, because his hour had not yet come.
verses.explainer: He speaks these words near the place where the offerings were put, in the Temple courts. Yet no one seizes him because his hour has not yet come. The 'hour' again controls events.

verses.text:
Jesus said therefore again to them,
“I am going away, and you will seek me, and you will die in your sins. Where I go, you can’t come.”
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'I am going away, and you will look for me, and you will die in your sin. Where I go, you cannot come.' The stakes are now life and death -- rejecting Jesus means dying in sin.

verses.text:
The Jews therefore said, “Will he kill himself, because he says,
‘Where I am going, you can’t come’?”
verses.explainer: The Jews ask: 'Will he kill himself? Is that what he means by saying where I go you cannot come?' They consistently interpret his spiritual statements in physical, literal terms.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“You are from beneath. I am from above. You are of this world. I am not of this world.
verses.explainer: Jesus explains: 'You are from below; I am from above. You are of this world; I am not of this world.' The fundamental divide is not intellectual but ontological -- they are from different realms.

verses.text:
I said therefore to you that you will die in your sins; for unless you believe that I am
he, you will die in your sins.”
verses.explainer: If you do not believe that I am he, you will indeed die in your sins. The phrase 'I am he' (ego eimi) resonates with the divine name. Belief in Jesus' divine identity is the decisive issue.

verses.text:
They said therefore to him, “Who are you?”
Jesus said to them,
“Just what I have been saying to you from the beginning.
verses.explainer: They ask: 'Who are you?' Jesus answers: 'Just what I have been telling you from the beginning.' He has not been evasive; they have not been listening.

verses.text: I have many things to speak and to judge concerning you. However he who sent me is true; and the things which I heard from him, these I say to the world.”
verses.explainer: Jesus has much to say in judgment of them, but the one who sent him is trustworthy, and what Jesus has heard from him, he tells the world.

verses.text: They didn’t understand that he spoke to them about the Father.
verses.explainer: They do not understand that he is telling them about his Father. The misunderstanding runs deep.

verses.text:
Jesus therefore said to them,
“When you have lifted up the Son of Man, then you will know that I am he, and I do nothing of myself, but as my Father taught me, I say these things.
verses.explainer: When you have lifted up the Son of Man, then you will know that I am he. The 'lifting up' refers to the cross. The crucifixion will, paradoxically, reveal Jesus' true identity.

verses.text: He who sent me is with me. The Father hasn’t left me alone, for I always do the things that are pleasing to him.”
verses.explainer: The one who sent me is with me; he has not left me alone, for I always do what pleases him. Jesus' obedience to the Father is complete and unbroken.

verses.text: As he spoke these things, many believed in him.
verses.explainer: Even as he speaks, many believe in him. The divisive teaching produces faith in some, even as it hardens others.

verses.text:
Jesus therefore said to those Jews who had believed him,
“If you remain in my word, then you are truly my disciples.
verses.explainer: To the Jews who had believed him, Jesus says: 'If you hold to my teaching, you are really my disciples.' Initial belief must lead to ongoing commitment. Faith that does not continue is not genuine faith.

verses.text: You will know the truth, and the truth will make you free.”
verses.explainer: Then you will know the truth, and the truth will set you free. One of the most quoted verses in Western culture. Truth is not abstract philosophy but a person (Jesus) and his teaching. Freedom comes through knowing and following that truth.

thenVsNow.then: For a Jewish audience, 'freedom' immediately evoked the Exodus from Egypt. Jesus is offering a new liberation -- not from Pharaoh but from sin.
thenVsNow.now: This verse appears on university buildings and courthouse walls worldwide, often stripped of its religious context. In John, the truth that sets free is specifically the truth about and from Jesus.

verses.text:
They answered him, “We are Abraham’s offspring, and have never been in bondage to anyone. How do you say,
‘You will be made free’?”
verses.explainer: They protest: 'We are Abraham's descendants and have never been slaves of anyone. How can you say we will be set free?' The claim is historically absurd -- Israel had been enslaved by Egypt, Babylon, Persia, Greece, and was currently under Rome.

verses.text:
Jesus answered them,
“Most certainly I tell you, everyone who commits sin is the bondservant of sin.
verses.explainer: Jesus replies: 'Everyone who sins is a slave to sin.' The slavery he is talking about is internal, not political. Sin is a master that controls behavior from within.

verses.text: A bondservant doesn’t live in the house forever. A son remains forever.
verses.explainer: A slave has no permanent place in the household, but a son belongs to it forever. The distinction between slave status and family status foreshadows the offer.

verses.text: If therefore the Son makes you free, you will be free indeed.
verses.explainer: So if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed. True freedom is not self-liberation but liberation by the Son. Only one with authority in the household can grant permanent freedom.

verses.text: I know that you are Abraham’s offspring, yet you seek to kill me, because my word finds no place in you.
verses.explainer: Jesus acknowledges they are Abraham's descendants physically but says they are ready to kill him because there is no room in their hearts for his word. Biological descent means nothing if the heart is closed.

verses.text: I say the things which I have seen with my Father; and you also do the things which you have seen with your father.”
verses.explainer: I am telling you what I have seen in the Father's presence, and you are doing what you have heard from your father. Jesus distinguishes between his Father (God) and their father -- a distinction they will not appreciate.

verses.text:
They answered him, “Our father is Abraham.”
Jesus said to them,
“If you were Abraham’s children, you would do the works of Abraham.
verses.explainer: They insist: 'Abraham is our father.' Jesus responds: 'If you were Abraham's children, you would do what Abraham did.' Abraham believed God; they reject God's Son. Their actions contradict their claimed ancestry.

verses.text: But now you seek to kill me, a man who has told you the truth, which I heard from God. Abraham didn’t do this.
verses.explainer: As it is, you are trying to kill me, a man who has told you the truth that I heard from God. Abraham did not do such things. Abraham welcomed divine visitors (Genesis 18); they try to kill God's messenger.

verses.text:
You do the works of your father.”
They said to him, “We were not born of sexual immorality. We have one Father, God.”
verses.explainer: You are doing the works of your own father. They protest: 'We are not illegitimate children. The only Father we have is God himself.' The accusation of illegitimacy may be a veiled slur against Jesus' birth.

verses.text:
Therefore Jesus said to them,
“If God were your father, you would love me, for I came out and have come from God. For I haven’t come of myself, but he sent me.
verses.explainer: If God were your Father, you would love me, for I have come here from God. I have not come on my own; God sent me. The test of claiming God as Father is whether you love God's Son.

verses.text: Why don’t you understand my speech? Because you can’t hear my word.
verses.explainer: Why is my language not clear to you? Because you are unable to hear what I say. The inability is not intellectual but spiritual. They cannot process truth because their spiritual hearing is broken.

verses.text: You are of your father, the devil, and you want to do the desires of your father. He was a murderer from the beginning, and doesn’t stand in the truth, because there is no truth in him. When he speaks a lie, he speaks on his own; for he is a liar, and the father of lies.
verses.explainer: You belong to your father, the devil, and you want to carry out your father's desires. He was a murderer from the beginning, not holding to the truth. When he lies, he speaks his native language, for he is a liar and the father of lies. This is the most severe accusation Jesus makes in the Gospels. He identifies their spiritual father as Satan.

verses.text: But because I tell the truth, you don’t believe me.
verses.explainer: Yet because I tell the truth, you do not believe me. Truth itself is the obstacle. They reject him precisely because he tells the truth.

verses.text: Which of you convicts me of sin? If I tell the truth, why do you not believe me?
verses.explainer: Can any of you prove me guilty of sin? If I am telling the truth, why do you not believe me? Jesus challenges them to find a single fault. The silence is deafening.

verses.text: He who is of God hears the words of God. For this cause you don’t hear, because you are not of God.”
verses.explainer: Whoever belongs to God hears what God says. The reason you do not hear is that you do not belong to God. The verdict is clear: their inability to receive truth reveals their spiritual allegiance.

verses.text: Then the Jews answered him, “Don’t we say well that you are a Samaritan, and have a demon?”
verses.explainer: They retort: 'Aren't we right in saying that you are a Samaritan and demon-possessed?' These are the worst insults available -- heretic and lunatic.

verses.text:
Jesus answered,
“I don’t have a demon, but I honor my Father, and you dishonor me.
verses.explainer: Jesus calmly denies the demon possession: 'I am not possessed by a demon, but I honor my Father and you dishonor me.' He does not even address the Samaritan slur.

verses.text: But I don’t seek my own glory. There is one who seeks and judges.
verses.explainer: I am not seeking glory for myself; but there is one who seeks it, and he is the judge. Jesus' vindication comes from God, not from winning arguments.

verses.text: Most certainly, I tell you, if a person keeps my word, he will never see death.”
verses.explainer: Whoever obeys my word will never see death. This is a staggering promise -- not immunity from physical death but triumph over it. Death will not have the final word.

verses.text:
Then the Jews said to him, “Now we know that you have a demon. Abraham died, and the prophets; and you say,
‘If a man keeps my word, he will never taste of death.’
verses.explainer: They seize on this: 'Now we know that you are demon-possessed! Abraham died and so did the prophets, yet you say that whoever obeys your word will never taste death.' The claim seems to contradict the universal human experience of death.

verses.text: Are you greater than our father, Abraham, who died? The prophets died. Who do you make yourself out to be?”
verses.explainer: Are you greater than our father Abraham? He died, and so did the prophets. Who do you think you are? The question is meant to be rhetorical. The answer Jesus is about to give will shock them.

verses.text:
Jesus answered,
“If I glorify myself, my glory is nothing. It is my Father who glorifies me, of whom you say that he is our God.
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'If I glorify myself, my glory means nothing. My Father, whom you claim as your God, is the one who glorifies me.'

verses.text: You have not known him, but I know him. If I said, ‘I don’t know him,’ I would be like you, a liar. But I know him, and keep his word.
verses.explainer: Though you do not know him, I know him. If I said I did not, I would be a liar like you. Jesus claims intimate knowledge of God and accuses them of not knowing the God they claim to worship.

verses.text: Your father Abraham rejoiced to see my day. He saw it, and was glad.”
verses.explainer: Your father Abraham rejoiced at the thought of seeing my day; he saw it and was glad. Abraham looked forward to Jesus' coming and rejoiced. Jesus claims that Abraham himself anticipated the Messiah.

verses.text: The Jews therefore said to him, “You are not yet fifty years old, and have you seen Abraham?”
verses.explainer: The Jews protest: 'You are not yet fifty years old, and you have seen Abraham!' They hear a chronological absurdity.

verses.text:
Jesus said to them,
“Most certainly, I tell you, before Abraham came into existence, I AM.”
verses.explainer: Jesus answers: 'Very truly I tell you, before Abraham was born, I AM.' This is the climactic claim of the chapter and one of the most important verses in the Gospel. Jesus does not say 'I was' (which would claim mere pre-existence) but 'I AM' (which claims the divine name). He identifies himself with the God of Exodus 3:14.

thenVsNow.then: The audience understood exactly what Jesus was claiming. By saying 'I AM' in this context, he was not just claiming age but claiming to be God. Their response (attempting to stone him) shows they understood this as blasphemy.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is central to the Christian doctrine of Christ's divinity. It leaves no room for viewing Jesus as merely a great teacher or prophet -- he either is who he claims to be, or the claim itself disqualifies him from being a good teacher.

verses.text: Therefore they took up stones to throw at him, but Jesus was hidden, and went out of the temple, having gone through the middle of them, and so passed by.
verses.explainer: At this, they pick up stones to stone him. Stoning was the prescribed punishment for blasphemy. They understood the claim perfectly. But Jesus hides himself and leaves the Temple. His hour has not yet come.

explainer.summary: Jesus heals a man born blind -- the sixth sign -- by making mud and sending him to wash in the Pool of Siloam. The healing on the Sabbath triggers an extended investigation by the Pharisees that reads like a courtroom drama. As the blind man's spiritual sight increases, the religious leaders' spiritual blindness deepens. The chapter ends with a devastating irony: those who claim to see are the truly blind ones.
themes: The sixth sign: sight for the man born blind
Sin, suffering, and the works of God
I AM the light of the world (continued)
Progressive revelation and growing faith
Spiritual blindness of the religious leaders
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter dramatizes the light/darkness theme that runs through the entire Gospel. The man born blind moves from physical darkness to physical sight to spiritual sight. The Pharisees move from claimed spiritual sight to exposed spiritual blindness. The two trajectories cross in the middle.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The Pool of Siloam, where Jesus sends the blind ma
wordStudies.meaning: The Pool of Siloam, where Jesus sends the blind man to wash. John notes that the name means 'Sent,' connecting it to Jesus who is the one 'sent' by the Father. The blind man goes to the pool of 'Sent' because the one who is 'sent' told him to.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A formal excommunication from the synagogue commun
wordStudies.meaning: A formal excommunication from the synagogue community. This was a devastating social punishment that cut a person off from religious life, community support, and economic networks. By the time John wrote, this was happening to Jewish Christians.

verses.text: As he passed by, he saw a man blind from birth.
verses.explainer: As Jesus goes along, he sees a man blind from birth. The condition is congenital and total -- this man has never seen anything. The severity of the case makes the miracle all the more remarkable.

verses.text: His disciples asked him, “Rabbi, who sinned, this man or his parents, that he was born blind?”
verses.explainer: The disciples ask: 'Rabbi, who sinned, this man or his parents, that he was born blind?' The question assumes a direct connection between specific sin and specific suffering -- a common theological framework in the ancient world.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Sin and suffering', 'explanation': "Ancient Judaism debated whether suffering was always punishment for sin. Some rabbis even suggested a person could sin in the womb. The disciples' question reflects this widespread assumption that disability must be someone's fault."}

verses.text:
Jesus answered,
“Neither did this man sin, nor his parents; but, that the works of God might be revealed in him.
verses.explainer: Jesus rejects both options: 'Neither this man nor his parents sinned. This happened so that the works of God might be displayed in him.' Jesus reframes suffering from punishment to purpose. The blindness is not a verdict but an opportunity for God to act.

thenVsNow.then: The assumption that suffering always signals sin was deeply embedded in ancient culture. Jesus' rejection of this framework was revolutionary -- it disconnected disability from guilt.
thenVsNow.now: The question of why good people suffer remains one of the most difficult theological problems. Jesus' answer does not explain all suffering but offers a different lens: suffering can become the occasion for God's work to be displayed.

verses.text: I must work the works of him who sent me, while it is day. The night is coming, when no one can work.
verses.explainer: As long as it is day, we must do the works of the one who sent me. Night is coming, when no one can work. The urgency of Jesus' mission is framed in terms of limited time -- his 'day' of public ministry will end at the cross.

verses.text: While I am in the world, I am the light of the world.”
verses.explainer: While I am in the world, I am the light of the world. Jesus connects this healing directly to his claim in 8:12. Giving sight to a blind man is the physical demonstration of the spiritual reality.

verses.text: When he had said this, he spat on the ground, made mud with the saliva, anointed the blind man’s eyes with the mud,
verses.explainer: Jesus spits on the ground, makes mud with the saliva, and puts it on the man's eyes. The use of mud echoes Genesis 2, where God formed humanity from the ground. Jesus is performing an act of new creation.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Mud and saliva', 'explanation': "Saliva was believed to have healing properties in the ancient world. But making mud on the Sabbath could be classified as 'kneading,' one of the 39 prohibited categories of Sabbath work. Jesus deliberately performs the healing in the most provocative way possible."}

verses.text:
and said to him,
“Go, wash in the pool of Siloam” (which means “Sent”). So he went away, washed, and came back seeing.
verses.explainer: Jesus tells him: 'Go, wash in the Pool of Siloam' (which means Sent). So the man goes, washes, and comes back seeing. He obeys without seeing -- a model of faith. The pool's name underscores the theology: the one 'sent' by God sends the blind man to 'Sent.'

verses.text: The neighbors therefore, and those who saw that he was blind before, said, “Isn’t this he who sat and begged?”
verses.explainer: The man's neighbors and those who had formerly seen him begging ask: 'Isn't this the same man who used to sit and beg?' His transformation is so complete that people question his identity.

verses.text:
Others were saying, “It is he.” Still others were saying, “He looks like him.”
He said, “I am he.”
verses.explainer: Some say yes, others say he just looks like him. But the man himself insists: 'I am the man.' His self-identification is clear even as others doubt.

verses.text: They therefore were asking him, “How were your eyes opened?”
verses.explainer: They demand to know how his eyes were opened. The interrogation begins with the neighbors and will escalate to the Pharisees.

verses.text:
He answered, “A man called Jesus made mud, anointed my eyes, and said to me,
‘Go to the pool of Siloam, and wash.’ So I went away and washed, and I received sight.”
verses.explainer: He replies: 'The man they call Jesus made some mud and put it on my eyes. He told me to go to Siloam and wash. So I went and washed, and then I could see.' His account is straightforward and factual.

verses.text:
Then they asked him, “Where is he?”
He said, “I don’t know.”
verses.explainer: They ask: 'Where is this man?' He says: 'I don't know.' The healed man does not yet know who healed him -- his understanding will grow progressively through the chapter.

verses.text: They brought him who had been blind to the Pharisees.
verses.explainer: They bring the man to the Pharisees. The healing has become a community event that requires official adjudication.

verses.text: It was a Sabbath when Jesus made the mud and opened his eyes.
verses.explainer: Now the day on which Jesus had made the mud and opened the man's eyes was a Sabbath. John mentions this only now, after the miracle is already established. The Sabbath detail transforms a miracle into a controversy.

verses.text: Again therefore the Pharisees also asked him how he received his sight. He said to them, “He put mud on my eyes, I washed, and I see.”
verses.explainer: The Pharisees ask how he received his sight. He tells them: 'He put mud on my eyes, and I washed, and now I see.' The testimony is consistent and simple.

verses.text: Some therefore of the Pharisees said, “This man is not from God, because he doesn’t keep the Sabbath.” Others said, “How can a man who is a sinner do such signs?” There was division among them.
verses.explainer: Some Pharisees say: 'This man is not from God, for he does not keep the Sabbath.' But others ask: 'How can a sinner do such signs?' They are divided among themselves. The miracle creates a logical problem they cannot resolve.

thenVsNow.then: The Pharisees were genuinely committed to the Sabbath as God's command. Their dilemma was real: either Jesus was a Sabbath-breaker (and therefore a sinner) or the miracle was from God (and therefore Jesus was not a sinner). They could not hold both.
thenVsNow.now: This pattern recurs whenever institutional religion encounters genuine spiritual power operating outside institutional channels. The evidence does not fit the categories, forcing a choice between adjusting the categories and denying the evidence.

verses.text:
Therefore they asked the blind man again, “What do you say about him, because he opened your eyes?”
He said, “He is a prophet.”
verses.explainer: They turn back to the blind man: 'What do you say about him? It was your eyes he opened.' He says: 'He is a prophet.' The man's understanding is growing -- from 'the man they call Jesus' to 'a prophet.' By the chapter's end, he will worship Jesus as Lord.

verses.text: The Jews therefore did not believe concerning him, that he had been blind, and had received his sight, until they called the parents of him who had received his sight,
verses.explainer: The Jews still do not believe that he had been blind and had received his sight. They call for his parents to verify his identity.

verses.text: and asked them, “Is this your son, whom you say was born blind? How then does he now see?”
verses.explainer: They ask the parents: 'Is this your son? Is he the one you say was born blind? How is it that now he can see?' Three questions designed to find a crack in the story.

verses.text: His parents answered them, “We know that this is our son, and that he was born blind;
verses.explainer: The parents confirm: 'We know he is our son, and we know he was born blind.' They verify the facts.

verses.text: but how he now sees, we don’t know; or who opened his eyes, we don’t know. He is of age. Ask him. He will speak for himself.”
verses.explainer: But they dodge the third question: 'How he can see now, or who opened his eyes, we don't know. Ask him. He is of age; he will speak for himself.' They throw their own son under the bus to protect themselves.

verses.text: His parents said these things because they feared the Jews; for the Jews had already agreed that if any man would confess him as Christ, he would be put out of the synagogue.
verses.explainer: His parents say this because they are afraid of the Jewish leaders, who have already decided that anyone who acknowledges Jesus as the Messiah will be put out of the synagogue. Fear of excommunication silences the parents.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Excommunication from the synagogue', 'explanation': 'Being put out of the synagogue (aposynagogos) meant total social exclusion -- loss of community, economic relationships, and religious participation. By the time John wrote (c. 85-95 AD), this was being applied to Jewish Christians who confessed Jesus as Messiah, making this detail painfully relevant to the original audience.'}

verses.text: Therefore his parents said, “He is of age. Ask him.”
verses.explainer: That was why his parents said 'He is of age; ask him.' Parental love gives way to self-preservation. The social cost of associating with Jesus is too high.

verses.text: So they called the man who was blind a second time, and said to him, “Give glory to God. We know that this man is a sinner.”
verses.explainer: A second time they summon the man who had been blind. 'Give glory to God by telling the truth,' they say. 'We know this man is a sinner.' They have reached their verdict before completing the investigation.

verses.text: He therefore answered, “I don’t know if he is a sinner. One thing I do know: that though I was blind, now I see.”
verses.explainer: He replies: 'Whether he is a sinner or not, I don't know. One thing I do know: I was blind but now I see!' This is one of the most powerful testimonies in the Bible -- simple, undeniable, and impossible to argue away.

verses.text: They said to him again, “What did he do to you? How did he open your eyes?”
verses.explainer: They ask again: 'What did he do to you? How did he open your eyes?' They are fishing for contradictions in his testimony.

verses.text: He answered them, “I told you already, and you didn’t listen. Why do you want to hear it again? You don’t also want to become his disciples, do you?”
verses.explainer: He answers with growing boldness: 'I have told you already and you did not listen. Why do you want to hear it again? Do you want to become his disciples too?' The formerly helpless beggar is now mocking the religious authorities.

verses.text: They insulted him and said, “You are his disciple, but we are disciples of Moses.
verses.explainer: They hurl insults at him: 'You are this fellow's disciple! We are disciples of Moses!' Their appeal to Moses will backfire -- Moses himself pointed to Jesus (5:46).

verses.text: We know that God has spoken to Moses. But as for this man, we don’t know where he comes from.”
verses.explainer: We know that God spoke to Moses, but as for this fellow, we do not even know where he comes from. Ironically, not knowing where Jesus comes from is exactly their problem -- he comes from God.

verses.text: The man answered them, “How amazing! You don’t know where he comes from, yet he opened my eyes.
verses.explainer: The man replies with devastating logic: 'Now that is remarkable! You don't know where he comes from, yet he opened my eyes.' The gap between the evidence and their conclusion is absurd.

verses.text: We know that God doesn’t listen to sinners, but if anyone is a worshiper of God, and does his will, he listens to him.
verses.explainer: We know that God does not listen to sinners. He listens to the godly person who does his will. The man is now doing theology better than the theologians.

verses.text: Since the world began it has never been heard of that anyone opened the eyes of someone born blind.
verses.explainer: Nobody has ever heard of opening the eyes of a man born blind. This miracle is unprecedented in the entire history of Israel -- no prophet, no priest, no king ever did this.

verses.text: If this man were not from God, he could do nothing.”
verses.explainer: If this man were not from God, he could do nothing. The logic is complete: an unprecedented miracle requires a divine source. The uneducated beggar has outreasoned the theological experts.

verses.text: They answered him, “You were altogether born in sins, and do you teach us?” They threw him out.
verses.explainer: They respond: 'You were steeped in sin at birth; how dare you lecture us!' They fall back on the very theology Jesus rejected in verse 3 -- that his blindness proves his sinfulness. Then they throw him out.

thenVsNow.then: Being expelled from the synagogue meant losing one's entire social network and religious community. For a former beggar who had just received his sight, this was trading one form of exclusion for another.
thenVsNow.now: The pattern of institutional religion expelling those whose experience threatens its categories is one of the most persistent dynamics in religious history. The man who received sight is punished for seeing too clearly.

verses.text:
Jesus heard that they had thrown him out, and finding him, he said,
“Do you believe in the Son of God?”
verses.explainer: Jesus hears that they have thrown him out, finds him, and asks: 'Do you believe in the Son of Man?' Jesus seeks out the expelled man. When the institution rejects someone, Jesus goes looking for them.

verses.text: He answered, “Who is he, Lord, that I may believe in him?”
verses.explainer: The man asks: 'Who is he, sir? Tell me so that I may believe in him.' He is willing to believe but needs to know who the Son of Man is.

verses.text:
Jesus said to him,
“You have both seen him, and it is he who speaks with you.”
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'You have now seen him; in fact, he is the one speaking with you.' The man who was born blind now sees the Son of Man face to face. The physical sight has led to spiritual sight.

verses.text: He said, “Lord, I believe!” and he worshiped him.
verses.explainer: The man says 'Lord, I believe,' and worships Jesus. This is the climax of his progressive journey: from 'the man they call Jesus' to 'a prophet' to 'Lord' to worship. His faith has reached its destination.

verses.text:
Jesus said,
“I came into this world for judgment, that those who don’t see may see; and that those who see may become blind.”
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'For judgment I came into this world, so that the blind will see and those who see will become blind.' The great reversal: physical blindness healed, spiritual blindness exposed.

verses.text: Those of the Pharisees who were with him heard these things, and said to him, “Are we also blind?”
verses.explainer: Some Pharisees who are with him hear this and ask: 'What? Are we blind too?' They sense the accusation but resist it.

verses.text:
Jesus said to them,
“If you were blind, you would have no sin; but now you say, ‘We see.’ Therefore your sin remains.
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'If you were blind, you would not be guilty of sin; but now that you claim you can see, your guilt remains.' Honest ignorance can be forgiven; willful blindness cannot. Their claim to see makes them accountable for what they refuse to see.

explainer.summary: Jesus delivers the Good Shepherd discourse, containing two more 'I AM' statements: 'I am the gate for the sheep' and 'I am the good shepherd.' He describes the intimate relationship between shepherd and sheep, declares he has 'other sheep' not of this fold, and asserts that he lays down his life voluntarily -- no one takes it from him. At the Feast of Dedication (Hanukkah), the confrontation with the Jewish leaders intensifies until they try to stone him for claiming 'I and the Father are one.'
themes: I AM the gate (third I AM statement)
I AM the good shepherd (fourth I AM statement)
Voluntary sacrifice: laying down his life
Other sheep: Gentile inclusion
I and the Father are one
explainer.contextInBook: The shepherd imagery follows naturally from chapter 9, where the Pharisees acted as bad shepherds who expelled one of their own flock. Jesus contrasts their leadership with his own -- they exploit the sheep; he dies for them.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The word 'good' (kalos) means not just morally goo
wordStudies.meaning: The word 'good' (kalos) means not just morally good but beautiful, noble, ideal. Jesus is not merely an adequate shepherd but the model shepherd, the shepherd par excellence. The image draws on Psalm 23, Ezekiel 34, and Israel's entire tradition of God as shepherd.

wordStudies.translatedAs: When Jesus says 'I and the Father are one,' the Gr
wordStudies.meaning: When Jesus says 'I and the Father are one,' the Greek word is hen (neuter) not heis (masculine). This indicates unity of essence and purpose rather than identity of person. The Father and Son are not the same person but share the same nature and will.

verses.text: “Most certainly, I tell you, one who doesn’t enter by the door into the sheep fold, but climbs up some other way, the same is a thief and a robber.
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'Very truly I tell you, anyone who does not enter the sheep pen by the gate but climbs in by some other way is a thief and a robber.' The image is immediately recognizable: someone bypassing the proper entrance has criminal intent.

verses.text: But one who enters in by the door is the shepherd of the sheep.
verses.explainer: The one who enters by the gate is the shepherd of the sheep. Legitimate shepherds use the front door. The contrast with the Pharisees of chapter 9 is implicit.

verses.text: The gatekeeper opens the gate for him, and the sheep listen to his voice. He calls his own sheep by name, and leads them out.
verses.explainer: The gatekeeper opens the gate for him, and the sheep listen to his voice. He calls his own sheep by name and leads them out. The intimacy is striking -- he knows each sheep individually and they recognize his voice.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Calling sheep by name', 'explanation': "Palestinian shepherds in the first century (and still today) gave individual names to their sheep and could call them out from a mixed flock by voice alone. The sheep responded only to their own shepherd's voice, ignoring strangers."}

verses.text: Whenever he brings out his own sheep, he goes before them, and the sheep follow him, for they know his voice.
verses.explainer: When he has brought out all his own, he goes on ahead of them, and his sheep follow him because they know his voice. The shepherd leads from the front, not from behind. This contrasts with Western shepherding where dogs drive sheep; Eastern shepherds walk ahead and the flock follows.

verses.text: They will by no means follow a stranger, but will flee from him; for they don’t know the voice of strangers.”
verses.explainer: They will not follow a stranger; in fact, they will run away from him because they do not recognize a stranger's voice. The sheep's safety depends on their ability to distinguish the genuine shepherd's voice from all others.

verses.text: Jesus spoke this parable to them, but they didn’t understand what he was telling them.
verses.explainer: Jesus uses this figure of speech, but the Pharisees do not understand what he is telling them. The metaphor that should be obvious to anyone familiar with shepherding is opaque to them.

verses.text:
Jesus therefore said to them again,
“Most certainly, I tell you, I am the sheep’s door.
verses.explainer: Jesus says again: 'Very truly I tell you, I am the gate for the sheep.' The third 'I AM' statement. Jesus is not just the shepherd -- he is also the gate. Access to the flock comes only through him.

verses.text: All who came before me are thieves and robbers, but the sheep didn’t listen to them.
verses.explainer: All who have come before me are thieves and robbers, but the sheep have not listened to them. Jesus is not condemning the Old Testament prophets but the false leaders who exploit the flock for personal gain.

verses.text: I am the door. If anyone enters in by me, he will be saved, and will go in and go out, and will find pasture.
verses.explainer: I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved. They will come in and go out and find pasture. Through Jesus, the sheep find salvation, freedom of movement, and sustenance. The three basic needs of any flock.

verses.text: The thief only comes to steal, kill, and destroy. I came that they may have life, and may have it abundantly.
verses.explainer: The thief comes only to steal and kill and destroy; I have come that they may have life, and have it to the full. The contrast could not be starker. False leaders take; the good shepherd gives. And what he gives is not mere survival but abundant, overflowing life.

thenVsNow.then: The religious leaders of Jesus' day used their position for social prestige and economic benefit. The prophets had long condemned such leaders (Ezekiel 34). Jesus draws on this tradition to indict the current establishment.
thenVsNow.now: The promise of 'life to the full' (or 'abundant life') is one of the most frequently cited verses in Christian spirituality. It redefines the purpose of faith -- not restriction but flourishing.

verses.text:
I am the good shepherd.
The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep.
verses.explainer: I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep. The fourth 'I AM' statement. What distinguishes the good shepherd from all others is his willingness to die for the flock. This is the voluntary sacrifice at the heart of the Gospel.

verses.text: He who is a hired hand, and not a shepherd, who doesn’t own the sheep, sees the wolf coming, leaves the sheep, and flees. The wolf snatches the sheep, and scatters them.
verses.explainer: The hired hand is not the shepherd and does not own the sheep. When he sees the wolf coming, he abandons the sheep and runs away. The hired hand works for wages, not love. When the cost becomes personal, he disappears.

verses.text: The hired hand flees because he is a hired hand, and doesn’t care for the sheep.
verses.explainer: The man runs away because he is a hired hand and cares nothing for the sheep. The indictment of self-serving religious leadership is sharp.

verses.text: I am the good shepherd. I know my own, and I’m known by my own;
verses.explainer: I am the good shepherd; I know my sheep and my sheep know me. The relationship is mutual and intimate -- not just the shepherd knowing the flock, but the flock knowing the shepherd.

verses.text: even as the Father knows me, and I know the Father. I lay down my life for the sheep.
verses.explainer: Just as the Father knows me and I know the Father -- and I lay down my life for the sheep. The intimacy between shepherd and sheep mirrors the intimacy between Father and Son. This is an astonishing parallel -- human-divine relationship modeled on intra-Trinitarian love.

verses.text:
I have other sheep, which are not of this fold.
I must bring them also, and they will hear my voice. They will become one flock with one shepherd.
verses.explainer: I have other sheep that are not of this sheep pen. I must bring them also. They too will listen to my voice, and there shall be one flock and one shepherd. The 'other sheep' are the Gentiles. Jesus' mission extends beyond Israel to encompass all peoples in one flock.

thenVsNow.then: For a Jewish audience, the inclusion of 'other sheep' was a radical expansion of God's covenant purposes. The Gentiles were not an afterthought but part of the plan from the beginning.
thenVsNow.now: This verse has been foundational for Christian mission. The 'one flock, one shepherd' vision continues to inspire efforts toward both evangelism and Christian unity across ethnic and denominational lines.

verses.text:
Therefore the Father loves me, because I lay down my life,

that I may take it again.
verses.explainer: The reason my Father loves me is that I lay down my life -- only to take it up again. The voluntary nature of Jesus' death is emphasized. It is not a tragedy imposed on him but a deliberate act of love.

verses.text: No one takes it away from me, but I lay it down by myself. I have power to lay it down, and I have power to take it again. I received this commandment from my Father.”
verses.explainer: No one takes it from me, but I lay it down of my own accord. I have authority to lay it down and authority to take it up again. This command I received from my Father. The crucifixion will look like an execution, but it is actually a self-sacrifice. Jesus goes to the cross not as a victim but as a volunteer.

verses.text: Therefore a division arose again among the Jews because of these words.
verses.explainer: The Jews who heard these words are once again divided. Division is the constant response to Jesus in John.

verses.text: Many of them said, “He has a demon, and is insane! Why do you listen to him?”
verses.explainer: Many say: 'He is demon-possessed and raving mad. Why listen to him?' The most dismissive response: write him off as crazy.

verses.text: Others said, “These are not the sayings of one possessed by a demon. It isn’t possible for a demon to open the eyes of the blind, is it?”
verses.explainer: But others say: 'These are not the sayings of a man possessed by a demon. Can a demon open the eyes of the blind?' The healing of the man born blind (chapter 9) remains the unanswerable evidence.

verses.text: It was the Feast of the Dedication at Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Then came the Festival of Dedication at Jerusalem. It was winter. The Feast of Dedication (Hanukkah) celebrated the rededication of the Temple by Judas Maccabeus in 164 BC after its desecration by Antiochus Epiphanes.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Festival of Dedication (Hanukkah)', 'explanation': "Celebrated in December, this festival commemorated the Maccabean victory over the Seleucid Empire and the rededication of the Temple in 164 BC. It was associated with the miracle of the oil that burned for eight days. The festival's themes of divine deliverance and Temple rededication provide a fitting backdrop for Jesus' claims."}

verses.text: It was winter, and Jesus was walking in the temple, in Solomon’s porch.
verses.explainer: Jesus is in the Temple courts, walking in Solomon's Colonnade. This covered portico on the eastern side of the Temple provided shelter from the winter weather.

verses.text: The Jews therefore came around him and said to him, “How long will you hold us in suspense? If you are the Christ, tell us plainly.”
verses.explainer: The Jews gather around him and demand: 'How long will you keep us in suspense? If you are the Messiah, tell us plainly.' They want a direct yes or no.

verses.text:
Jesus answered them,
“I told you, and you don’t believe. The works that I do in my Father’s name, these testify about me.
verses.explainer: Jesus answers: 'I did tell you, but you do not believe. The works I do in my Father's name testify about me.' He has told them -- through his works, his 'I AM' statements, and his discourses. The problem is not lack of information but lack of faith.

verses.text: But you don’t believe, because you are not of my sheep, as I told you.
verses.explainer: But you do not believe because you are not my sheep. Faith is not a human achievement but a gift related to belonging. Those who are his sheep hear his voice.

verses.text: My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me.
verses.explainer: My sheep listen to my voice; I know them, and they follow me. The three marks of belonging: hearing, being known, and following.

verses.text: I give eternal life to them. They will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand.
verses.explainer: I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish; no one will snatch them out of my hand. One of the strongest assurance passages in the Bible. Eternal life is a gift, perdition is impossible, and the grip of Jesus cannot be broken.

thenVsNow.then: In a world of precarious existence -- political instability, disease, poverty, persecution -- the promise that no one could snatch believers from Jesus' hand offered a security that nothing else could match.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is central to debates about eternal security ('once saved, always saved'). Whatever one's theological position, the assurance of being held in Jesus' hand remains one of the most comforting images in the New Testament.

verses.text: My Father, who has given them to me, is greater than all. No one is able to snatch them out of my Father’s hand.
verses.explainer: My Father, who has given them to me, is greater than all; no one can snatch them out of my Father's hand. Double security: held in the Son's hand, which is inside the Father's hand. The protection is absolute.

verses.text: I and the Father are one.”
verses.explainer: I and the Father are one. The climactic claim. Jesus asserts unity with God -- not merely of purpose but of nature. The Greek word 'one' (hen) is neuter, indicating oneness of essence rather than identity of person.

thenVsNow.then: This statement precipitated an immediate attempt to stone Jesus. The audience understood it as a claim to deity, not merely to prophetic authority or messianic status.
thenVsNow.now: This verse became foundational for the Christian doctrine of the Trinity -- the Father and Son are distinct persons who share one divine nature. Centuries of theological reflection were needed to articulate what Jesus stated in five words.

verses.text: Therefore Jews took up stones again to stone him.
verses.explainer: Again his Jewish opponents pick up stones to stone him. This is the second stoning attempt (after 8:59). The pattern of escalating violence continues.

verses.text:
Jesus answered them,
“I have shown you many good works from my Father. For which of those works do you stone me?”
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'I have shown you many good works from the Father. For which of these do you stone me?' He forces them to name the offense. The works themselves are undeniably good.

verses.text: The Jews answered him, “We don’t stone you for a good work, but for blasphemy: because you, being a man, make yourself God.”
verses.explainer: They answer: 'We are not stoning you for any good work but for blasphemy, because you, a mere man, claim to be God.' They understand his claim perfectly. The issue is not his works but his identity.

verses.text:
Jesus answered them,
“Isn’t it written in your law, ‘I said, you are gods?’
verses.explainer: Jesus responds by quoting Psalm 82:6: 'Is it not written in your Law, I have said you are gods?' He uses their own Scripture to create logical space for his claim.

verses.text: If he called them gods, to whom the word of God came (and the Scripture can’t be broken),
verses.explainer: If he called them 'gods' to whom the word of God came -- and Scripture cannot be set aside -- then how can they accuse him of blasphemy for saying 'I am God's Son' when the Father consecrated him and sent him into the world?

verses.text: do you say of him whom the Father sanctified and sent into the world, ‘You blaspheme,’ because I said, ‘I am the Son of God?’
verses.explainer: Jesus' argument is careful: do not believe me just because of what I say. But if I do the Father's works, believe the works. The evidence is in the actions.

verses.text: If I don’t do the works of my Father, don’t believe me.
verses.explainer: Do not believe me unless I do the works of my Father. Jesus puts the burden of proof on himself -- his works must match his claims.

verses.text: But if I do them, though you don’t believe me, believe the works; that you may know and believe that the Father is in me, and I in the Father.”
verses.explainer: But if I do them, even though you do not believe me, believe the works, that you may know and understand that the Father is in me, and I in the Father. The mutual indwelling of Father and Son -- the deepest mystery of the Gospel -- is stated plainly.

verses.text: They sought again to seize him, and he went out of their hand.
verses.explainer: Again they try to seize him, but he escapes their grasp. Third attempt to take him. He remains free because his hour has not come.

verses.text: He went away again beyond the Jordan into the place where John was baptizing at first, and there he stayed.
verses.explainer: Jesus goes back across the Jordan to the place where John had been baptizing in the early days. He returns to where his public ministry began -- a narrative full circle.

verses.text: Many came to him. They said, “John indeed did no sign, but everything that John said about this man is true.”
verses.explainer: Many people come to him. They say: 'Though John never performed a sign, all that John said about this man was true.' The Baptist's witness is vindicated by Jesus' works.

verses.text: Many believed in him there.
verses.explainer: And in that place many believe in him. Away from Jerusalem's hostility, in the countryside where John the Baptist first pointed to Jesus, faith flourishes. The chapter ends on a note of quiet triumph.

explainer.summary: The raising of Lazarus is the climactic seventh sign in John's Gospel. When Jesus' close friend Lazarus falls ill, Jesus deliberately delays his return to Bethany until Lazarus has been dead four days. Martha meets Jesus on the road and confesses her faith; Mary falls at his feet weeping. Jesus himself weeps at the tomb before commanding Lazarus to come out. The miracle triggers the final decision by the Sanhedrin to kill Jesus -- the one who gives life becomes the target of death. Caiaphas unwittingly prophesies that Jesus will die for the nation.
themes: The seventh and greatest sign
Jesus as the resurrection and the life
The humanity of Jesus (weeping)
Faith in the face of death
Caiaphas' unwitting prophecy
Life that leads to death -- the irony of the plot against Jesus
explainer.contextInBook: This is the turning point of the entire Gospel. The seven signs are now complete, and each has revealed a different facet of Jesus' glory. The raising of Lazarus demonstrates power over death itself -- the final enemy. But this supreme act of life-giving directly causes the plot that will lead to Jesus' own death. The irony is quintessentially Johannine: giving life costs life.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Literally 'standing up again
wordStudies.meaning: Literally 'standing up again.' Martha understood resurrection as a future event at the end of time. Jesus redefines it as a present person: 'I AM the resurrection and the life.' The hope is not an event on a timeline but a relationship with a living person.

wordStudies.translatedAs: When Jesus sees Mary weeping, he is 'deeply moved
wordStudies.meaning: When Jesus sees Mary weeping, he is 'deeply moved in spirit' (embrimaomai) -- a word that suggests anger or indignation, not just sadness. He is also 'troubled' (tarassō). The combination suggests Jesus is angry at death itself, at the devastation sin has brought into the world, even as he grieves with those he loves.

verses.text: Now a certain man was sick, Lazarus from Bethany, of the village of Mary and her sister, Martha.
verses.explainer: A man named Lazarus is sick. He is from Bethany, the village of Mary and Martha. John introduces the crisis by naming the village before the characters, grounding the story geographically.

verses.text: It was that Mary who had anointed the Lord with ointment, and wiped his feet with her hair, whose brother, Lazarus, was sick.
verses.explainer: This Mary is identified as the one who will later anoint Jesus with perfume and wipe his feet with her hair. John references an event he has not yet narrated (chapter 12), assuming his readers already know the tradition.

verses.text: The sisters therefore sent to him, saying, “Lord, behold, he for whom you have great affection is sick.”
verses.explainer: The sisters send word to Jesus: 'Lord, the one you love is sick.' They do not ask for healing -- they simply state the need and trust Jesus' love to respond. The appeal is relational, not transactional.

verses.text:
But when Jesus heard it, he said,
“This sickness is not to death, but for the glory of God, that God’s Son may be glorified by it.”
verses.explainer: When Jesus hears this, he says: 'This sickness will not end in death. No, it is for God's glory so that God's Son may be glorified through it.' The statement is paradoxically true -- Lazarus will die, but death will not have the last word.

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient world, serious illness was frequently a death sentence. The idea that sickness could serve a purpose beyond itself -- revealing divine glory -- would have been both comforting and bewildering.
thenVsNow.now: The tension between trusting God's purposes and experiencing real suffering remains one of the deepest challenges of faith. Jesus does not prevent the suffering; he redeems it.

verses.text: Now Jesus loved Martha, and her sister, and Lazarus.
verses.explainer: Now Jesus loved Martha and her sister and Lazarus. John emphasizes the love before narrating the delay. What follows is not indifference but intentional.

verses.text: When therefore he heard that he was sick, he stayed two days in the place where he was.
verses.explainer: So when he heard that Lazarus was sick, he stayed where he was two more days. The word 'so' is jarring -- 'he loved them, so he delayed.' Jesus' love does not always look like immediate rescue. The delay ensures Lazarus will be unmistakably dead, making the miracle undeniable.

verses.text:
Then after this he said to the disciples,
“Let’s go into Judea again.”
verses.explainer: After the two days, Jesus says to his disciples: 'Let us go back to Judea.' Judea is where the authorities want to kill him. Going back means walking toward his own death.

verses.text: The disciples told him, “Rabbi, the Jews were just trying to stone you, and are you going there again?”
verses.explainer: The disciples protest: 'Rabbi, a short while ago the Jews there tried to stone you, and yet you are going back?' Their concern is practical and reasonable.

verses.text:
Jesus answered,
“Aren’t there twelve hours of daylight? If a man walks in the day, he doesn’t stumble, because he sees the light of this world.
verses.explainer: Jesus answers: 'Are there not twelve hours of daylight? Anyone who walks in the daytime will not stumble, for they see by this world's light.' He is speaking of his appointed time -- he has a fixed number of 'hours' to accomplish his work, and until that time is up, no one can stop him.

verses.text: But if a man walks in the night, he stumbles, because the light isn’t in him.”
verses.explainer: It is when a person walks at night that they stumble, for they have no light. Walking outside God's will is walking in darkness. Jesus walks in the light of the Father's plan.

verses.text:
He said these things, and after that, he said to them,
“Our friend, Lazarus, has fallen asleep, but I am going so that I may awake him out of sleep.”
verses.explainer: Jesus tells them: 'Our friend Lazarus has fallen asleep; but I am going there to wake him up.' Jesus uses 'sleep' as a metaphor for death -- a metaphor that implies death is temporary and reversible for those connected to him.

verses.text: The disciples therefore said, “Lord, if he has fallen asleep, he will recover.”
verses.explainer: The disciples reply: 'Lord, if he sleeps, he will get better.' They take Jesus literally, thinking Lazarus is merely resting and will recover naturally.

verses.text: Now Jesus had spoken of his death, but they thought that he spoke of taking rest in sleep.
verses.explainer: John explains: Jesus had been speaking of Lazarus' death, but they thought he meant natural sleep. The misunderstanding is typical of John's Gospel, where surface-level reading always misses the deeper meaning.

verses.text:
So Jesus said to them plainly then,
“Lazarus is dead.
verses.explainer: So then he tells them plainly: 'Lazarus is dead.' No more metaphor. The blunt statement prepares them for what is coming.

verses.text: I am glad for your sakes that I was not there, so that you may believe. Nevertheless, let’s go to him.”
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'And for your sake I am glad I was not there, so that you may believe. But let us go to him.' Jesus' gladness is not about Lazarus' death but about what the disciples are about to witness -- a revelation that will deepen their faith beyond anything they have seen.

verses.text: Thomas therefore, who is called Didymus, said to his fellow disciples, “Let’s go also, that we may die with him.”
verses.explainer: Thomas (called Didymus, meaning Twin) says to the other disciples: 'Let us also go, that we may die with him.' Thomas expects death, not resurrection. But his willingness to die with Jesus is a courage that is often overlooked in the shadow of his later doubt.

verses.text: So when Jesus came, he found that he had been in the tomb four days already.
verses.explainer: On his arrival, Jesus finds that Lazarus has already been in the tomb for four days. The four-day detail is crucial -- by Jewish belief, the soul departed the body permanently after three days. This death is beyond any possibility of resuscitation.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Four days in the tomb', 'explanation': 'Jewish tradition held that the soul hovered near the body for three days, hoping to return. After three days, decomposition made it clear the death was irreversible. By arriving on the fourth day, Jesus faces a death that is absolutely final by every human and religious measure.'}

verses.text: Now Bethany was near Jerusalem, about fifteen stadia away.
verses.explainer: Bethany is less than two miles from Jerusalem. The proximity to Jerusalem means the Jewish leaders will quickly learn of whatever happens here.

verses.text: Many of the Jews had joined the women around Martha and Mary, to console them concerning their brother.
verses.explainer: Many Jews have come to Martha and Martha to comfort them in the loss of their brother. The mourning customs bring a significant audience to witness what is about to occur.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Jewish mourning customs', 'explanation': 'The formal mourning period (shiva) lasted seven days. Friends and neighbors were obligated to visit, sit with the bereaved, and offer comfort. Professional mourners were sometimes hired. The house would be full of visitors, which is why many witnesses are present when Jesus arrives.'}

verses.text: Then when Martha heard that Jesus was coming, she went and met him, but Mary stayed in the house.
verses.explainer: When Martha hears that Jesus is coming, she goes out to meet him, but Mary stays at home. The contrast between the two sisters is consistent with their portraits in Luke 10 -- Martha is active and goes; Mary sits.

verses.text: Therefore Martha said to Jesus, “Lord, if you would have been here, my brother wouldn’t have died.
verses.explainer: Martha says to Jesus: 'Lord, if you had been here, my brother would not have died.' This is a statement of faith in what Jesus could have done, mixed with the anguish of what he did not do. It is grief speaking truth.

verses.text: Even now I know that, whatever you ask of God, God will give you.”
verses.explainer: But I know that even now God will give you whatever you ask. Martha's faith stretches beyond disappointment. She does not know what to expect but believes Jesus has access to God's power even in this impossible situation.

verses.text:
Jesus said to her,
“Your brother will rise again.”
verses.explainer: Jesus says to her: 'Your brother will rise again.' The statement is deliberately ambiguous -- it could refer to the general resurrection at the end of time or to something about to happen.

verses.text: Martha said to him, “I know that he will rise again in the resurrection at the last day.”
verses.explainer: Martha replies: 'I know he will rise again in the resurrection at the last day.' She gives the standard Pharisaic answer -- correct theology, but focused on the distant future rather than the present moment.

verses.text:
Jesus said to her,
“I am the resurrection and the life. He who believes in me will still live, even if he dies.
verses.explainer: Jesus declares: 'I am the resurrection and the life. The one who believes in me will live, even though they die.' This is the fifth 'I AM' statement. Jesus does not say he will give resurrection at some future date. He says he IS the resurrection. The hope is a person, not a calendar event.

annotations.word: I am the resurrection and the life

thenVsNow.then: The Pharisees believed in a future resurrection at the end of the age. The Sadducees denied resurrection entirely. Jesus transcends both positions by locating resurrection in his own person, available now.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is read at more funerals than perhaps any other. The claim is staggering: physical death does not end the life of anyone who trusts Jesus. The resurrection is not just a doctrine to affirm but a person to know.

verses.text: Whoever lives and believes in me will never die. Do you believe this?”
verses.explainer: And whoever lives by believing in me will never die. Do you believe this? Jesus presses Martha to a personal response. Theology must become faith. She cannot remain a spectator.

verses.text: She said to him, “Yes, Lord. I have come to believe that you are the Christ, God’s Son, he who comes into the world.”
verses.explainer: Martha answers: 'Yes, Lord, I believe that you are the Messiah, the Son of God, who is to come into the world.' Her confession is one of the most complete christological statements in the Gospels, rivaling Peter's confession in the Synoptics. A woman delivers it.

thenVsNow.then: Martha's confession -- 'the Messiah, the Son of God, who is to come' -- packs three messianic titles into one sentence. That a woman makes this declaration, in a culture where women's testimony was devalued, is profoundly significant.
thenVsNow.now: Martha is often remembered as the busy sister from Luke 10. John's Gospel reveals her as a profound theologian whose confession of faith is among the most important in the New Testament.

verses.text: When she had said this, she went away, and called Mary, her sister, secretly, saying, “The Teacher is here, and is calling you.”
verses.explainer: After saying this, Martha goes back and calls her sister Mary aside. 'The Teacher is here,' she says, 'and is asking for you.' Martha becomes a messenger, bringing Mary to Jesus.

verses.text: When she heard this, she arose quickly, and went to him.
verses.explainer: When Mary hears this, she gets up quickly and goes to him.

verses.text: Now Jesus had not yet come into the village, but was in the place where Martha met him.
verses.explainer: Jesus has not yet entered the village but is still at the place where Martha had met him. He waits outside, perhaps to avoid the crowd of mourners and have a private encounter.

verses.text: Then the Jews who were with her in the house, and were consoling her, when they saw Mary, that she rose up quickly and went out, followed her, saying, “She is going to the tomb to weep there.”
verses.explainer: The Jews who have been comforting Mary in the house notice her leave quickly and follow her, assuming she is going to the tomb to mourn there. Their assumption brings more witnesses to the scene.

verses.text: Therefore when Mary came to where Jesus was, and saw him, she fell down at his feet, saying to him, “Lord, if you would have been here, my brother wouldn’t have died.”
verses.explainer: When Mary reaches Jesus, she falls at his feet and says: 'Lord, if you had been here, my brother would not have died.' The same words as Martha, but the posture is different -- Mary falls at Jesus' feet. Both sisters share the same grief and the same faith.

verses.text: When Jesus therefore saw her weeping, and the Jews weeping who came with her, he groaned in the spirit, and was troubled,
verses.explainer: When Jesus sees her weeping, and the Jews who have come along with her also weeping, he is deeply moved in spirit and troubled. The Greek embrimaomai suggests an intense emotional response -- not just sympathy but indignation. Jesus is angry at the devastation death brings to those he loves.

verses.text:
and said,
“Where have you laid him?”
They told him, “Lord, come and see.”
verses.explainer: He asks: 'Where have you laid him?' They reply: 'Come and see, Lord.' Jesus asks what he already knows, drawing the community into the journey toward the miracle.

verses.text: Jesus wept.
verses.explainer: Jesus wept. The shortest verse in the Bible and one of the most profound. The Greek word (edakrysen) indicates silent tears -- not the loud wailing of the mourners but quiet weeping. The one who is about to raise Lazarus from the dead still grieves. Omnipotence does not cancel compassion.

thenVsNow.then: In a culture where stoic composure was valued, particularly for religious leaders, Jesus' public weeping was remarkable. He does not hide his grief or treat it as weakness.
thenVsNow.now: This verse has comforted countless people in grief. The God who can overcome death still weeps with those who suffer it. Power and compassion coexist perfectly in Jesus.

verses.text: The Jews therefore said, “See how much affection he had for him!”
verses.explainer: The Jews say: 'See how he loved him!' Even those who do not believe in Jesus recognize the depth of his love. Grief is a universal language.

verses.text: Some of them said, “Couldn’t this man, who opened the eyes of him who was blind, have also kept this man from dying?”
verses.explainer: But some of them say: 'Could not he who opened the eyes of the blind man have kept this man from dying?' The question is understandable -- if Jesus could heal a man born blind, why not prevent a friend's death? They do not yet realize that Jesus has something far greater in mind than prevention.

verses.text: Jesus therefore, again groaning in himself, came to the tomb. Now it was a cave, and a stone lay against it.
verses.explainer: Jesus, once more deeply moved, comes to the tomb. It is a cave with a stone laid across the entrance. The physical details ground the miracle in tangible reality -- a real cave, a real stone, a real dead body.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Rock-cut tombs', 'explanation': "Wealthy families in first-century Judea used natural caves or rock-cut chambers as tombs. Bodies were laid on stone shelves, and a heavy rolling stone sealed the entrance. After the flesh decomposed, the bones were gathered into an ossuary (bone box). Lazarus' family was affluent enough for such a tomb."}

verses.text:
Jesus said,
“Take away the stone.”
Martha, the sister of him who was dead, said to him, “Lord, by this time there is a stench, for he has been dead four days.”
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'Take away the stone.' Martha, the sister of the dead man, objects: 'But Lord, by this time there is a bad odor, for he has been there four days.' Martha's objection is practical and realistic. Decomposition is underway. She believes in future resurrection but struggles with present-tense intervention.

thenVsNow.then: Martha's reference to the smell of decomposition is brutally honest and medically accurate. After four days in the warm climate of Judea, the body would be in obvious decay.
thenVsNow.now: Martha's objection represents the tension between theological belief and practical doubt. She affirms resurrection in theory but struggles when confronted with the stench of reality. Faith is tested most severely at the tomb.

verses.text:
Jesus said to her,
“Didn’t I tell you that if you believed, you would see God’s glory?”
verses.explainer: Jesus replies: 'Did I not tell you that if you believe, you will see the glory of God?' Faith precedes sight. Jesus asks Martha to trust before she sees the evidence.

verses.text:
