So they took away the stone from the place where the dead man was lying. Jesus lifted up his eyes, and said,
“Father, I thank you that you listened to me.
verses.explainer: So they take away the stone. Jesus looks up and says: 'Father, I thank you that you have heard me.' The prayer is thanksgiving, not petition. Jesus has already communicated with the Father. The prayer is for the audience's benefit.

verses.text: I know that you always listen to me, but because of the multitude standing around I said this, that they may believe that you sent me.”
verses.explainer: I knew that you always hear me, but I said this for the benefit of the people standing here, that they may believe that you sent me. Jesus makes the prayer's purpose explicit -- it is a public demonstration that his power comes from the Father, not from himself.

verses.text:
When he had said this, he cried with a loud voice,
“Lazarus, come out!”
verses.explainer: Jesus calls out in a loud voice: 'Lazarus, come out!' The command is spoken to a dead man. The same voice that spoke creation into existence now speaks death into retreat. An early church father noted that if Jesus had not named Lazarus specifically, every dead person in the cemetery would have come out.

verses.text:
He who was dead came out, bound hand and foot with wrappings, and his face was wrapped around with a cloth.
Jesus said to them,
“Free him, and let him go.”
verses.explainer: The dead man comes out, his hands and feet wrapped with strips of linen, and a cloth around his face. Jesus says to them: 'Take off the grave clothes and let him go.' Lazarus emerges still bound in burial wrappings -- proof that this is a real body that was really dead and really buried. The community must participate in his liberation by removing the cloths.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Burial wrappings', 'explanation': 'Jewish burial practice involved washing the body, anointing it with spices to counter the smell of decay, and wrapping it in linen strips (similar to bandages). The face was covered with a separate cloth (soudarion). Lazarus emerges still bound, shuffling out of the tomb in a scene that is both triumphant and eerie.'}

verses.text: Therefore many of the Jews, who came to Mary and saw what Jesus did, believed in him.
verses.explainer: Therefore many of the Jews who had come to visit Mary, and had seen what Jesus did, believed in him. The sign accomplishes its purpose for many -- they see the glory and believe.

verses.text: But some of them went away to the Pharisees, and told them the things which Jesus had done.
verses.explainer: But some of them go to the Pharisees and tell them what Jesus has done. The response to the same miracle divides: some believe, some report to the authorities. The same evidence produces opposite conclusions.

verses.text: The chief priests therefore and the Pharisees gathered a council, and said, “What are we doing? For this man does many signs.
verses.explainer: Then the chief priests and the Pharisees call a meeting of the Sanhedrin. 'What are we accomplishing?' they ask. 'Here is this man performing many signs.' They acknowledge the miracles openly -- their problem is not with the evidence but with its implications.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'The Sanhedrin', 'explanation': 'The ruling council of seventy-one members, presided over by the high priest. It included both Sadducees (who controlled the priesthood) and Pharisees. They had authority over religious matters and limited civil authority under Roman supervision. An emergency session indicates the severity of the perceived threat.'}

verses.text: If we leave him alone like this, everyone will believe in him, and the Romans will come and take away both our place and our nation.”
verses.explainer: If we let him go on like this, everyone will believe in him, and then the Romans will come and take away both our Temple and our nation. The fear is political: a popular messianic movement could provoke Roman military intervention. The leaders choose institutional survival over truth.

thenVsNow.then: The fear was not irrational. Rome had destroyed other Jewish movements with brutal efficiency. The leaders calculated that one man's death was preferable to national catastrophe. Ironically, the nation was destroyed anyway in 70 AD.
thenVsNow.now: The temptation to sacrifice truth for institutional security is perennial. Religious and political leaders in every era face the choice between protecting the system and following where the evidence leads.

verses.text: But a certain one of them, Caiaphas, being high priest that year, said to them, “You know nothing at all,
verses.explainer: Then Caiaphas, the high priest that year, speaks: 'You know nothing at all!' His tone is contemptuous -- he considers the council incompetent hand-wringers.

verses.text: nor do you consider that it is advantageous for us that one man should die for the people, and that the whole nation not perish.”
verses.explainer: He says: 'You do not realize that it is better for you that one man die for the people than that the whole nation perish.' This is cold political calculus -- utilitarian reasoning at its most ruthless. Kill one to save the many.

verses.text: Now he didn’t say this of himself, but being high priest that year, he prophesied that Jesus would die for the nation,
verses.explainer: He did not say this on his own, but as high priest that year he prophesied that Jesus would die for the Jewish nation. John reveals that Caiaphas, despite his cynical motives, spoke prophetic truth. The high priest's office carried prophetic significance even when the man was unworthy of it.

verses.text: and not for the nation only, but that he might also gather together into one the children of God who are scattered abroad.
verses.explainer: And not only for that nation but also for the scattered children of God, to bring them together and make them one. John extends Caiaphas' prophecy beyond Israel to the global church -- Jesus' death will gather all God's people, Jew and Gentile, into one flock.

verses.text: So from that day forward they took counsel that they might put him to death.
verses.explainer: So from that day on they plot to take his life. The decision is made. The seventh sign -- the supreme demonstration of life-giving power -- has sealed Jesus' death sentence. The giver of life must die.

verses.text: Jesus therefore walked no more openly among the Jews, but departed from there into the country near the wilderness, to a city called Ephraim. He stayed there with his disciples.
verses.explainer: Therefore Jesus no longer moves about publicly among the people of Judea. Instead he withdraws to a region near the wilderness, to a village called Ephraim, where he stays with his disciples. Jesus retreats to wait for the appointed time -- the Passover.

verses.text: Now the Passover of the Jews was at hand. Many went up from the country to Jerusalem before the Passover, to purify themselves.
verses.explainer: When it is almost time for the Jewish Passover, many go up from the country to Jerusalem for their ceremonial cleansing before the Passover. The stage is being set for the final act.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Ceremonial cleansing before Passover', 'explanation': 'Pilgrims who had become ritually impure through contact with Gentiles, corpses, or other sources of impurity needed to undergo purification rites before eating the Passover meal. Many arrived in Jerusalem early to complete these rituals at the Temple.'}

verses.text: Then they sought for Jesus and spoke one with another, as they stood in the temple, “What do you think—that he isn’t coming to the feast at all?”
verses.explainer: They keep looking for Jesus, asking one another as they stand in the Temple courts: 'What do you think? Isn't he coming to the festival at all?' The question hangs in the air. The whole city is wondering.

verses.text: Now the chief priests and the Pharisees had commanded that if anyone knew where he was, he should report it, that they might seize him.
verses.explainer: But the chief priests and the Pharisees have given orders that anyone who finds out where Jesus is should report it so that they may arrest him. A warrant has been issued. The net is closing.

explainer.summary: Six days before Passover, Mary anoints Jesus' feet with expensive perfume -- an act of extravagant devotion that Jesus interprets as preparation for his burial. The next day, Jesus enters Jerusalem to the acclaim of palm-waving crowds, fulfilling Zechariah's prophecy. Greeks come seeking Jesus, triggering his declaration that 'the hour has come' for the Son of Man to be glorified. Jesus teaches that a grain of wheat must fall into the ground and die to bear fruit. A voice from heaven confirms him. Despite many signs, most of the Jewish leaders refuse to believe, fulfilling Isaiah's prophecy. The chapter closes with Jesus' final public appeal.
themes: Extravagant worship versus calculated giving
The triumphal entry and Zechariah's prophecy
The hour has come
The grain of wheat: death that produces life
Judgment of this world
Belief and unbelief
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 12 is the hinge of the Gospel. It closes the 'Book of Signs' (chapters 1-12) and prepares for the 'Book of Glory' (chapters 13-21). Jesus' public ministry ends here. From chapter 13 onward, he speaks only to his disciples and to those who arrest, try, and crucify him.

wordStudies.translatedAs: In John, 'glory' and 'glorification' refer to the
wordStudies.meaning: In John, 'glory' and 'glorification' refer to the cross itself, not just the resurrection that follows. The cross is where Jesus' true nature is most fully revealed -- self-giving love carried to its ultimate extreme. This inverts every human understanding of glory.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A double-meaning word in John
wordStudies.meaning: A double-meaning word in John. It refers both to being physically lifted up on a cross and to being exalted in glory. The crucifixion is simultaneously the lowest point of humiliation and the highest point of revelation.

verses.text: Then six days before the Passover, Jesus came to Bethany, where Lazarus was, who had been dead, whom he raised from the dead.
verses.explainer: Six days before the Passover, Jesus comes to Bethany, where Lazarus lives -- the one Jesus had raised from the dead. The narrative clock begins ticking toward the cross. Bethany is the base, and Lazarus is the living evidence.

verses.text: So they made him a supper there. Martha served, but Lazarus was one of those who sat at the table with him.
verses.explainer: A dinner is given in Jesus' honor. Martha serves, and Lazarus is among those reclining at the table. The ordinariness of the scene is striking -- a man raised from the dead simply eating dinner. The miraculous has become domestic.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Reclining at table', 'explanation': 'At formal meals in the Greco-Roman world, diners reclined on couches arranged around a central table, leaning on their left elbow and eating with their right hand. This posture signified leisure and fellowship. Lazarus, recently dead, now reclines at a banquet -- the ultimate picture of restored life.'}

verses.text: Mary, therefore, took a pound of ointment of pure nard, very precious, and anointed the feet of Jesus, and wiped his feet with her hair. The house was filled with the fragrance of the ointment.
verses.explainer: Mary takes about a pint of pure nard, an expensive perfume, and anoints Jesus' feet, wiping them with her hair. The house is filled with the fragrance. The act is extravagant -- a year's wages poured out in one gesture. Loosening her hair in public was culturally scandalous for a Jewish woman.

thenVsNow.then: For a woman to unbind her hair in public was considered shameful in Jewish culture -- it was an intimate act reserved for private settings. Mary's gesture transcends social propriety. Her worship is so focused on Jesus that she disregards what anyone else might think.
thenVsNow.now: Mary's anointing challenges the calculated, cost-benefit approach to worship. True devotion does not ask whether it is 'too much.' The fragrance that fills the house symbolizes how genuine worship overflows beyond the worshiper.

verses.text: Then Judas Iscariot, Simon’s son, one of his disciples, who would betray him, said,
verses.explainer: But Judas Iscariot, one of his disciples -- the one who would later betray him -- objects.

verses.text: “Why wasn’t this ointment sold for three hundred denarii, and given to the poor?”
verses.explainer: Judas asks: 'Why wasn't this perfume sold and the money given to the poor? It was worth a year's wages.' The objection sounds pious and practical.

verses.text: Now he said this, not because he cared for the poor, but because he was a thief, and having the money box, used to steal what was put into it.
verses.explainer: John exposes Judas' true motive: he did not say this because he cared about the poor but because he was a thief. As keeper of the money bag, he used to help himself to what was put into it. The narrator strips away the mask of concern to reveal naked greed.

verses.text:
But Jesus said,
“Leave her alone. She has kept this for the day of my burial.
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'Leave her alone. It was intended that she should save this perfume for the day of my burial.' Jesus interprets Mary's act prophetically -- she is anointing him for death, even if she does not fully realize it.

verses.text: For you always have the poor with you, but you don’t always have me.”
verses.explainer: You will always have the poor among you, but you will not always have me. Jesus is not dismissing the poor. He is acknowledging that his physical presence is temporary and that this is a unique, unrepeatable moment of devotion.

verses.text: A large crowd therefore of the Jews learned that he was there, and they came, not for Jesus’ sake only, but that they might see Lazarus also, whom he had raised from the dead.
verses.explainer: Meanwhile a large crowd of Jews finds out that Jesus is there and comes, not only because of him but also to see Lazarus, whom he has raised from the dead. Lazarus has become a tourist attraction -- living proof of Jesus' power.

verses.text: But the chief priests conspired to put Lazarus to death also,
verses.explainer: So the chief priests make plans to kill Lazarus as well. The evidence against their position must be eliminated. They cannot refute the miracle, so they decide to destroy the evidence -- a chilling display of institutional self-preservation.

verses.text: because on account of him many of the Jews went away and believed in Jesus.
verses.explainer: For on account of Lazarus many of the Jews are going over to Jesus and believing in him. Lazarus' continued existence is an ongoing evangelistic event. Every breath he takes testifies to Jesus' power.

verses.text: On the next day a great multitude had come to the feast. When they heard that Jesus was coming to Jerusalem,
verses.explainer: The next day the great crowd that has come for the festival hears that Jesus is on his way to Jerusalem. The Passover pilgrims prepare to welcome him.

verses.text: they took the branches of the palm trees, and went out to meet him, and cried out, “Hosanna! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord, the King of Israel!”
verses.explainer: They take palm branches and go out to meet him, shouting: 'Hosanna! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord! Blessed is the king of Israel!' The palm branches and the cry of Hosanna ('Save us!') echo the celebration of the Maccabean liberation. The crowd sees Jesus as a political liberator.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Palm branches', 'explanation': 'Palm branches were a national symbol of Jewish independence, associated with the Maccabean revolt of 164 BC when Judas Maccabeus entered the Temple in triumph. Waving palms was a political act -- the crowd was welcoming Jesus as a revolutionary king who would overthrow the Romans.'}

verses.text: Jesus, having found a young donkey, sat on it. As it is written,
verses.explainer: Jesus finds a young donkey and sits on it, as it is written in Scripture. His choice of mount is deliberate -- a donkey, not a war horse.

verses.text: “Don’t be afraid, daughter of Zion. Behold, your King comes, sitting on a donkey’s colt.”
verses.explainer: 'Do not be afraid, Daughter Zion; see, your king is coming, seated on a donkey's colt.' The quote from Zechariah redefines kingship. A king on a donkey comes in peace, not conquest. Jesus accepts the title of king but rejects the crowd's definition of kingship.

verses.text: His disciples didn’t understand these things at first, but when Jesus was glorified, then they remembered that these things were written about him, and that they had done these things to him.
verses.explainer: At first his disciples do not understand all this. Only after Jesus is glorified do they realize that these things had been written about him and had been done to him. Understanding comes after the resurrection. The disciples' comprehension is always retrospective in John.

verses.text: The multitude therefore that was with him when he called Lazarus out of the tomb, and raised him from the dead, was testifying about it.
verses.explainer: Now the crowd that was with him when he called Lazarus from the tomb and raised him from the dead continues to spread the word. The Lazarus miracle fuels the triumphal entry. The seventh sign directly connects to the final week.

verses.text: For this cause also the multitude went and met him, because they heard that he had done this sign.
verses.explainer: Many people go out to meet him because they hear he has performed this sign. The crowd grows because of testimony about Lazarus. Faith spreads through witness.

verses.text: The Pharisees therefore said among themselves, “See how you accomplish nothing. Behold, the world has gone after him.”
verses.explainer: The Pharisees say to one another: 'See, this is getting us nowhere. Look how the whole world has gone after him!' Their frustrated exaggeration is prophetically accurate -- the whole world will indeed come to Jesus, starting with the Greeks in the very next verse.

verses.text: Now there were certain Greeks among those that went up to worship at the feast.
verses.explainer: Now there are some Greeks among those who go up to worship at the festival. These are likely 'God-fearers' -- Gentiles who worship the God of Israel but have not fully converted to Judaism. Their arrival signals that Jesus' mission extends beyond Israel.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Greeks at the festival', 'explanation': "Gentiles who were attracted to Judaism ('God-fearers') could worship in the outer Court of the Gentiles at the Temple but could not enter the inner courts. Their request to see Jesus fulfills the Pharisees' just-spoken words: the whole world is coming to him."}

verses.text: These, therefore, came to Philip, who was from Bethsaida of Galilee, and asked him, saying, “Sir, we want to see Jesus.”
verses.explainer: They come to Philip, who is from Bethsaida in Galilee, with a request. 'Sir,' they say, 'we would like to see Jesus.' Philip has a Greek name and comes from a bilingual region, making him a natural intermediary.

verses.text: Philip came and told Andrew, and in turn, Andrew came with Philip, and they told Jesus.
verses.explainer: Philip tells Andrew; Andrew and Philip together tell Jesus. The two disciples with Greek names mediate between the Greek world and Jesus.

verses.text:
Jesus answered them,
“The time has come for the Son of Man to be glorified.
verses.explainer: Jesus replies: 'The hour has come for the Son of Man to be glorified.' Throughout the Gospel, Jesus has said 'my hour has not yet come' (2:4, 7:6, 7:30, 8:20). Now, triggered by the Gentiles' arrival, he declares the hour has finally arrived. The mission to the whole world requires the cross.

thenVsNow.then: In Jesus' context, 'glorification' meant being honored and exalted. But Jesus will redefine glory as self-sacrifice on the cross. The hour of glory is the hour of death -- a complete inversion of human values.
thenVsNow.now: The connection between the arrival of Gentile seekers and the triggering of 'the hour' reveals a pattern: the expansion of God's love to include all people requires the ultimate sacrifice. Inclusion costs everything.

verses.text: Most certainly I tell you, unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains by itself alone. But if it dies, it bears much fruit.
verses.explainer: Very truly I tell you, unless a grain of wheat falls to the ground and dies, it remains only a single seed. But if it dies, it produces many seeds. Jesus uses an agricultural parable to explain the necessity of his death. A seed that refuses to die remains alone; a seed that dies produces an abundant harvest.

thenVsNow.then: In an agricultural society, this metaphor was immediately understood. Every farmer knew that seed buried in the ground 'died' to produce a harvest many times larger than what was planted.
thenVsNow.now: The principle of fruitful death -- that new life requires letting go of the old -- runs through human experience from spiritual growth to creative endeavor. Holding on tightly to self produces isolation; releasing self produces abundance.

verses.text: He who loves his life will lose it. He who hates his life in this world will keep it to eternal life.
verses.explainer: Anyone who loves their life will lose it, while anyone who hates their life in this world will keep it for eternal life. Self-preservation is self-destruction; self-giving is self-preservation. The paradox is absolute.

verses.text: If anyone serves me, let him follow me. Where I am, there will my servant also be. If anyone serves me, the Father will honor him.
verses.explainer: Whoever serves me must follow me; and where I am, my servant also will be. My Father will honor the one who serves me. Following Jesus means going where he goes -- including through death to life.

verses.text: “Now my soul is troubled. What shall I say? ‘Father, save me from this time?’ But for this cause I came to this time.
verses.explainer: Now my soul is troubled, and what shall I say? 'Father, save me from this hour'? No, it was for this very reason I came to this hour. Jesus is deeply distressed -- this is John's equivalent of the Gethsemane agony. He considers asking to be spared but rejects the prayer. The hour is the purpose of his existence.

verses.text:
Father, glorify your name!”
Then there came a voice out of the sky, saying, “I have both glorified it, and will glorify it again.”
verses.explainer: Father, glorify your name! Then a voice comes from heaven: 'I have glorified it, and will glorify it again.' The Father's voice confirms that the cross -- the hour of apparent defeat -- is the moment of supreme glory. This is the third and final heavenly voice in the Gospels.

verses.text: The multitude therefore, who stood by and heard it, said that it had thundered. Others said, “An angel has spoken to him.”
verses.explainer: The crowd standing there hears it and says it has thundered; others say an angel has spoken to him. The same sound is interpreted differently depending on the hearer's receptivity. Some hear thunder; others hear a voice. The sign divides.

verses.text:
Jesus answered,
“This voice hasn’t come for my sake, but for your sakes.
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'This voice was for your benefit, not mine.' As with the prayer at Lazarus' tomb, the divine confirmation is for the witnesses, not for Jesus himself.

verses.text: Now is the judgment of this world. Now the prince of this world will be cast out.
verses.explainer: Now is the time for judgment on this world; now the prince of this world will be driven out. The cross is simultaneously judgment on evil and defeat of Satan. What looks like Jesus' defeat is actually the world's judgment and the devil's expulsion.

verses.text: And I, if I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself.”
verses.explainer: And I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself. 'Lifted up' refers simultaneously to crucifixion (physically elevated on the cross) and exaltation (spiritually glorified). The cross, the most repulsive instrument of execution, becomes the most attractive force in history.

verses.text: But he said this, signifying by what kind of death he should die.
verses.explainer: He says this to indicate the kind of death he is going to die. John explains the double meaning explicitly -- 'lifted up' means crucified.

verses.text:
The multitude answered him, “We have heard out of the law that the Christ remains forever. How do you say,
‘The Son of Man must be lifted up?’ Who is this Son of Man?”
verses.explainer: The crowd objects: 'We have heard from the Law that the Messiah will remain forever. How can you say the Son of Man must be lifted up? Who is this Son of Man?' The crowd knows the Messiah should be eternal but cannot reconcile that with death on a cross.

verses.text:
Jesus therefore said to them,
“Yet a little while the light is with you. Walk while you have the light, that darkness doesn’t overtake you. He who walks in the darkness doesn’t know where he is going.
verses.explainer: Jesus answers: 'You are going to have the light just a little while longer. Walk while you have the light, before darkness overtakes you.' His public ministry is ending. The light will soon be withdrawn, and they will face the consequences of how they responded to it.

verses.text: While you have the light, believe in the light, that you may become children of light.” Jesus said these things, and he departed and hid himself from them.
verses.explainer: Believe in the light while you have the light, so that you may become children of light. Jesus' final public appeal is urgent -- the window is closing. This is the last time he addresses the general public in John's Gospel.

verses.text: But though he had done so many signs before them, yet they didn’t believe in him,
verses.explainer: Even after Jesus has performed so many signs in their presence, they still do not believe in him. John steps back to assess the public ministry: abundant evidence, persistent unbelief.

verses.text:
that the word of Isaiah the prophet might be fulfilled, which he spoke,
“Lord, who has believed our report?
To whom has the arm of the Lord been revealed?”
verses.explainer: This is to fulfill the word of Isaiah the prophet: 'Lord, who has believed our message and to whom has the arm of the Lord been revealed?' Isaiah predicted that the servant's message would be met with unbelief.

verses.text: For this cause they couldn’t believe, for Isaiah said again,
verses.explainer: For this reason they could not believe, because, as Isaiah says elsewhere...

verses.text:
“He has blinded their eyes and he hardened their heart,
lest they should see with their eyes,
and perceive with their heart,
and would turn,
and I would heal them.”
verses.explainer: 'He has blinded their eyes and hardened their hearts, so they can neither see with their eyes, nor understand with their hearts, nor turn -- and I would heal them.' The hardening is both a divine judgment and a human choice. They refused to see, and eventually they lost the ability to see.

verses.text: Isaiah said these things when he saw his glory, and spoke of him.
verses.explainer: Isaiah said this because he saw Jesus' glory and spoke about him. John makes a stunning claim: when Isaiah saw the Lord 'high and exalted' in the Temple (Isaiah 6), he was seeing Jesus. The pre-incarnate Christ is the glory Isaiah beheld.

verses.text: Nevertheless even of the rulers many believed in him, but because of the Pharisees they didn’t confess it, so that they wouldn’t be put out of the synagogue,
verses.explainer: Yet at the same time many even among the leaders believe in him. But because of the Pharisees they do not openly acknowledge their faith for fear they will be put out of the synagogue. Belief exists among the elite but remains hidden -- crippled by the threat of social exclusion.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Put out of the synagogue', 'explanation': "Expulsion from the synagogue (aposynagogos) meant social death in a Jewish community. It cut a person off from worship, commerce, marriage prospects, and communal support. By John's time (c. 90 AD), a formal curse on Christians (birkat ha-minim) may have been added to synagogue liturgy, making this a lived reality for his readers."}

verses.text: for they loved men’s praise more than God’s praise.
verses.explainer: For they love human praise more than praise from God. John diagnoses the disease: they value human approval over divine approval. Fear of social consequences outweighs faith.

verses.text:
Jesus cried out and said,
“Whoever believes in me, believes not in me, but in him who sent me.
verses.explainer: Then Jesus cries out: 'Whoever believes in me does not believe in me only, but in the one who sent me.' Believing in Jesus means believing in the Father. The two are inseparable.

verses.text: He who sees me sees him who sent me.
verses.explainer: The one who looks at me is seeing the one who sent me. Jesus is the visible image of the invisible God. To see Jesus is to see the Father.

verses.text: I have come as a light into the world, that whoever believes in me may not remain in the darkness.
verses.explainer: I have come into the world as a light, so that no one who believes in me should stay in darkness. The light/darkness theme returns for Jesus' final public statement.

verses.text: If anyone listens to my sayings, and doesn’t believe, I don’t judge him. For I came not to judge the world, but to save the world.
verses.explainer: If anyone hears my words but does not keep them, I do not judge that person. For I did not come to judge the world, but to save the world. The first coming is for salvation, not condemnation.

verses.text: He who rejects me, and doesn’t receive my sayings, has one who judges him. The word that I spoke, the same will judge him in the last day.
verses.explainer: There is a judge for the one who rejects me and does not accept my words; the very words I have spoken will condemn them at the last day. The words themselves become the standard of judgment. Rejecting the message is self-judgment.

verses.text: For I spoke not from myself, but the Father who sent me, he gave me a commandment, what I should say, and what I should speak.
verses.explainer: For I did not speak on my own, but the Father who sent me commanded me to say all that I have spoken. Jesus' words carry the Father's authority. To reject them is to reject God.

verses.text: I know that his commandment is eternal life. The things therefore which I speak, even as the Father has said to me, so I speak.”
verses.explainer: I know that his command leads to eternal life. So whatever I say is just what the Father has told me to say. The final sentence of Jesus' public ministry reaffirms his total obedience to the Father. Everything he has said and done flows from this relationship.

explainer.summary: The 'Book of Glory' opens with the Last Supper. John does not record the institution of the Lord's Supper (the bread and wine); instead he narrates Jesus washing the disciples' feet -- an act of radical servanthood that redefines leadership. Jesus identifies Judas as the betrayer, gives him a morsel of bread, and Judas goes out 'into the night.' After the traitor's departure, Jesus gives the 'new commandment' to love one another as he has loved them, and predicts Peter's denial.
themes: Servant leadership exemplified in foot washing
The identification and departure of the betrayer
The new commandment: love one another
Peter's overconfidence and predicted denial
The transition from public ministry to private instruction
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 13 begins the Farewell Discourse (chapters 13-17), Jesus' extended private teaching to his disciples on the night before his death. This is the most intimate section of any Gospel -- Jesus preparing his closest followers for his departure, the coming of the Spirit, and the mission that will follow. The foot washing is the acted parable that governs everything that follows.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The love Jesus commands is agapē -- self-giving lo
wordStudies.meaning: The love Jesus commands is agapē -- self-giving love that seeks the good of others regardless of their worthiness. It is not an emotion but a commitment to action. Jesus defines it by example: he washes the feet of his betrayer. This is the love that will become the distinguishing mark of his followers.

wordStudies.translatedAs: When Judas goes out, John adds the ominous note: '
wordStudies.meaning: When Judas goes out, John adds the ominous note: 'And it was night.' In John's symbolic world, night represents separation from Jesus, the light of the world. Judas' departure into physical darkness mirrors his descent into spiritual darkness.

verses.text: Now before the feast of the Passover, Jesus, knowing that his time had come that he would depart from this world to the Father, having loved his own who were in the world, he loved them to the end.
verses.explainer: It is just before the Passover Festival. Jesus knows that the hour has come for him to leave this world and go to the Father. Having loved his own who are in the world, he loves them to the end. The Greek eis telos means both 'to the end' (in time) and 'to the uttermost' (in degree). His love reaches its fullest expression in what follows.

thenVsNow.then: The Passover commemorated Israel's liberation from slavery in Egypt. On this Passover eve, Jesus will enact a new liberation -- not from political bondage but from sin and death.
thenVsNow.now: The phrase 'to the end' challenges the conditional love that characterizes most human relationships. Jesus' love is not withdrawn when his followers fail, betray, or deny him. It persists through everything.

verses.text: During supper, the devil having already put into the heart of Judas Iscariot, Simon’s son, to betray him,
verses.explainer: The evening meal is in progress, and the devil has already prompted Judas, the son of Simon Iscariot, to betray Jesus. The cosmic conflict is laid bare -- behind Judas' decision stands the devil's influence. But Jesus acts in full knowledge of what is coming.

verses.text: Jesus, knowing that the Father had given all things into his hands, and that he came from God, and was going to God,
verses.explainer: Jesus knows that the Father has put all things under his power, and that he has come from God and is returning to God. This is the most important context for the foot washing: Jesus washes feet not because he is weak but because he is omnipotent. His authority is the foundation of his service.

verses.text: arose from supper, and laid aside his outer garments. He took a towel, and wrapped a towel around his waist.
verses.explainer: So he gets up from the meal, takes off his outer clothing, and wraps a towel around his waist. The Son of God strips down and dresses as a household slave. The action is as shocking to the disciples as it would be to see a king cleaning toilets.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Foot washing', 'explanation': "In the dusty roads of first-century Palestine, foot washing was necessary before reclining at a meal. It was the task of the lowest household slave -- so demeaning that a Jewish slave could not be required to do it. Only Gentile slaves, wives, children, or disciples of a rabbi (as an act of extreme devotion) would perform it. For a rabbi to wash his disciples' feet inverted every social expectation."}

verses.text: Then he poured water into the basin, and began to wash the disciples’ feet, and to wipe them with the towel that was wrapped around him.
verses.explainer: After that, he pours water into a basin and begins to wash his disciples' feet, drying them with the towel that is wrapped around him. The master becomes the servant. The creator of the universe kneels before his creatures.

verses.text: Then he came to Simon Peter. He said to him, “Lord, do you wash my feet?”
verses.explainer: He comes to Simon Peter, who says to him: 'Lord, are you going to wash my feet?' Peter's question carries horror and protest. The word order in Greek emphasizes the contrast: 'You -- my feet -- you wash?'

verses.text:
Jesus answered him,
“You don’t know what I am doing now, but you will understand later.”
verses.explainer: Jesus replies: 'You do not realize now what I am doing, but later you will understand.' The foot washing has a meaning deeper than hygiene -- it is an enacted parable of the cross, where Jesus will take the lowest place for the sake of others.

verses.text:
Peter said to him, “You will never wash my feet!”
Jesus answered him,
“If I don’t wash you, you have no part with me.”
verses.explainer: Peter says: 'No, you shall never wash my feet.' Jesus answers: 'Unless I wash you, you have no part with me.' Peter's refusal sounds humble but is actually resistance to grace. Accepting service from Jesus requires surrendering pride and self-sufficiency.

thenVsNow.then: Peter's refusal reflects the honor/shame culture's horror at reversed social roles. A disciple simply could not allow his master to perform a slave's task. To accept it meant accepting a fundamentally new understanding of authority.
thenVsNow.now: Peter's resistance mirrors the difficulty many people have in receiving grace -- the insistence on earning, deserving, or reciprocating rather than simply receiving. Jesus says relationship with him requires allowing him to serve you.

verses.text: Simon Peter said to him, “Lord, not my feet only, but also my hands and my head!”
verses.explainer: Simon Peter exclaims: 'Then, Lord, not just my feet but my hands and my head as well!' Peter swings from absolute refusal to excessive demand. His enthusiasm, while endearing, still misses the point.

verses.text:
Jesus said to him,
“Someone who has bathed only needs to have his feet washed, but is completely clean. You are clean, but not all of you.”
verses.explainer: Jesus answers: 'Those who have had a bath need only to wash their feet; their whole body is clean. And you are clean, though not every one of you.' The spiritual meaning emerges: the disciples have been cleansed through their association with Jesus (the 'bath'), but ongoing cleansing (foot washing) is still needed. The exception -- 'not every one of you' -- refers to Judas.

verses.text:
For he knew him who would betray him, therefore he said,
“You are not all clean.”
verses.explainer: For he knew who was going to betray him, and that was why he said not every one was clean. John clarifies: Jesus washes the feet of his betrayer, knowing full well what Judas is about to do. This is love extended to an enemy.

verses.text:
So when he had washed their feet, put his outer garment back on, and sat down again, he said to them,
“Do you know what I have done to you?
verses.explainer: When he finishes washing their feet, he puts on his clothes and returns to his place. 'Do you understand what I have done for you?' he asks. The lesson is about to be made explicit.

verses.text: You call me, ‘Teacher’ and ‘Lord.’ You say so correctly, for so I am.
verses.explainer: You call me 'Teacher' and 'Lord,' and rightly so, for that is what I am. Jesus affirms his authority. The foot washing does not diminish his status; it redefines what authority means.

verses.text: If I then, the Lord and the Teacher, have washed your feet, you also ought to wash one another’s feet.
verses.explainer: Now that I, your Lord and Teacher, have washed your feet, you also should wash one another's feet. The command is not optional. If the master serves, the servants must serve. Hierarchy does not disappear; it is redirected toward service.

thenVsNow.then: In Greco-Roman society, power meant being served by others. Jesus inverts this completely: the highest authority uses its power to serve those beneath it.
thenVsNow.now: The model of servant leadership that Jesus demonstrates here has influenced everything from Christian ministry to modern organizational theory. True leadership, Jesus says, is measured by willingness to serve.

verses.text: For I have given you an example, that you also should do as I have done to you.
verses.explainer: I have set you an example that you should do as I have done for you. The foot washing is both a literal practice (which some churches still observe) and a principle: sacrificial service for others.

verses.text: Most certainly I tell you, a servant is not greater than his lord, neither one who is sent greater than he who sent him.
verses.explainer: Very truly I tell you, no servant is greater than his master, nor is a messenger greater than the one who sent him. If the master washes feet, no follower can consider any service beneath them.

verses.text: If you know these things, blessed are you if you do them.
verses.explainer: Now that you know these things, you will be blessed if you do them. Knowledge without action is worthless. The blessing comes in the doing, not merely the knowing.

verses.text: I don’t speak concerning all of you. I know whom I have chosen. But that the Scripture may be fulfilled, ‘He who eats bread with me has lifted up his heel against me.’
verses.explainer: I am not referring to all of you; I know those I have chosen. But this is to fulfill this passage of Scripture: 'He who shared my bread has turned against me.' The betrayal is foreseen and scripturally anticipated. Even Judas' treachery operates within God's sovereign plan.

verses.text: From now on, I tell you before it happens, that when it happens, you may believe that I am he.
verses.explainer: I am telling you now before it happens, so that when it does happen you will believe that I am who I am. Predictive revelation serves faith -- when events unfold as Jesus said, the disciples will recognize his divine knowledge.

verses.text: Most certainly I tell you, he who receives whomever I send, receives me; and he who receives me, receives him who sent me.”
verses.explainer: Very truly I tell you, whoever accepts anyone I send accepts me; and whoever accepts me accepts the one who sent me. The chain of representation extends from the Father to Jesus to the disciples to anyone they reach.

verses.text:
When Jesus had said this, he was troubled in spirit, and testified,
“Most certainly I tell you that one of you will betray me.”
verses.explainer: After he has said this, Jesus is troubled in spirit and testifies: 'Very truly I tell you, one of you is going to betray me.' The distress is genuine -- Jesus is deeply affected by the betrayal, even though he has known about it from the beginning.

verses.text: The disciples looked at one another, perplexed about whom he spoke.
verses.explainer: His disciples stare at one another, at a loss to know which of them he means. The shock is palpable. None of them suspects Judas -- a reminder of how well he has hidden his intentions.

verses.text: One of his disciples, whom Jesus loved, was at the table, leaning against Jesus’ breast.
verses.explainer: One of them, the disciple whom Jesus loved, is reclining next to him. This is the first appearance of the 'beloved disciple,' who reclines in the position of honor, his head near Jesus' chest.

verses.text: Simon Peter therefore beckoned to him, and said to him, “Tell us who it is of whom he speaks.”
verses.explainer: Simon Peter motions to this disciple and says: 'Ask him which one he means.' Peter uses his proximity to the beloved disciple as a communication channel to Jesus.

verses.text: He, leaning back, as he was, on Jesus’ breast, asked him, “Lord, who is it?”
verses.explainer: Leaning back against Jesus, the disciple asks: 'Lord, who is it?' The physical intimacy of the scene underscores the beloved disciple's unique closeness to Jesus.

verses.text:
Jesus therefore answered,
“It is he to whom I will give this piece of bread when I have dipped it.” So when he had dipped the piece of bread, he gave it to Judas, the son of Simon Iscariot.
verses.explainer: Jesus answers: 'It is the one to whom I will give this piece of bread when I have dipped it in the dish.' Then, dipping the piece of bread, he gives it to Judas, the son of Simon Iscariot. Giving a morsel of bread dipped in the common dish was an act of honor and friendship. Jesus offers one final gesture of love to the one who will destroy him.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Dipping bread in the dish', 'explanation': "At a formal meal, the host would dip a piece of bread in the communal sauce dish and offer it to a favored guest as a mark of special honor and friendship. Jesus' gesture toward Judas is a final, gracious appeal -- even at the moment of identification, Jesus extends kindness to his betrayer."}

verses.text:
After the piece of bread, then Satan entered into him.
Then Jesus said to him,
“What you do, do quickly.”
verses.explainer: As soon as Judas takes the bread, Satan enters into him. Jesus says to him: 'What you are about to do, do quickly.' The moment of demonic possession coincides with the refusal of grace. Jesus does not prevent it; he authorizes it. The divine plan moves forward through human evil.

verses.text: Now no man at the table knew why he said this to him.
verses.explainer: But no one at the meal understands why Jesus says this to him. The other disciples have no idea what is happening between Jesus and Judas.

verses.text: For some thought, because Judas had the money box, that Jesus said to him, “Buy what things we need for the feast,” or that he should give something to the poor.
verses.explainer: Since Judas has charge of the money, some think Jesus is telling him to buy what is needed for the festival, or to give something to the poor. The innocent interpretation reveals how unsuspecting the disciples are.

verses.text: Therefore having received that morsel, he went out immediately. It was night.
verses.explainer: As soon as Judas takes the bread, he goes out. And it was night. Four of the most ominous words in Scripture. The physical darkness mirrors the spiritual darkness Judas is entering. He leaves the light of Jesus' presence and walks into the night from which he will never return.

thenVsNow.then: In a world without street lighting, going out at night meant entering complete darkness. John's symbolism is vivid: Judas leaves the one who called himself the light of the world and plunges into absolute darkness.
thenVsNow.now: The brevity and finality of 'and it was night' continues to haunt readers. It represents every choice to walk away from light into darkness, from truth into deception, from love into isolation.

verses.text:
When he had gone out, Jesus said,
“Now the Son of Man has been glorified, and God has been glorified in him.
verses.explainer: When Judas is gone, Jesus says: 'Now the Son of Man is glorified and God is glorified in him.' With the betrayer gone, Jesus can speak freely. The glorification has begun -- paradoxically, through the very act of betrayal that will lead to the cross.

verses.text: If God has been glorified in him, God will also glorify him in himself, and he will glorify him immediately.
verses.explainer: If God is glorified in him, God will glorify the Son in himself, and will glorify him at once. The mutual glorification of Father and Son is the heartbeat of John's theology. The cross reveals the Father's love; the resurrection reveals the Son's vindication.

verses.text: Little children, I will be with you a little while longer. You will seek me, and as I said to the Jews, ‘Where I am going, you can’t come,’ so now I tell you.
verses.explainer: My children, I will be with you only a little longer. You will look for me, and just as I told the Jews, so I tell you now: Where I am going, you cannot come. The term 'my children' (teknia) is unique to this moment -- a tender, parental address that reveals the depth of Jesus' affection.

verses.text: A new commandment I give to you, that you love one another. Just as I have loved you, you also love one another.
verses.explainer: A new commandment I give you: Love one another. As I have loved you, so you must love one another. The commandment to love is not new in itself (Leviticus 19:18). What is new is the standard: 'as I have loved you.' The standard is the cross -- self-sacrificing love without limits.

thenVsNow.then: The 'new commandment' is given on the night Jesus demonstrates its meaning by washing feet and heading toward the cross. The standard is not abstract; it is embodied in his actions.
thenVsNow.now: This commandment defines Christian community. The distinguishing mark of Jesus' followers is not their beliefs, rituals, or moral code but how they love one another. It remains the most challenging and most recognizable feature of authentic faith.

verses.text: By this everyone will know that you are my disciples, if you have love for one another.”
verses.explainer: By this everyone will know that you are my disciples, if you love one another. The identity marker of the church is not orthodoxy, power, or cultural influence but mutual love. Jesus says the world will recognize his followers by one thing only.

verses.text:
Simon Peter said to him, “Lord, where are you going?”
Jesus answered,
“Where I am going, you can’t follow now, but you will follow afterwards.”
verses.explainer: Simon Peter asks: 'Lord, where are you going?' Jesus replies: 'Where I am going, you cannot follow now, but you will follow later.' Peter will indeed follow -- to his own cross, according to tradition and the hint in chapter 21.

verses.text: Peter said to him, “Lord, why can’t I follow you now? I will lay down my life for you.”
verses.explainer: Peter declares: 'Lord, why can't I follow you now? I will lay down my life for you.' Peter's sincerity is not in question. His self-knowledge is.

verses.text:
Jesus answered him,
“Will you lay down your life for me? Most certainly I tell you, the rooster won’t crow until you have denied me three times.
verses.explainer: Jesus answers: 'Will you really lay down your life for me? Very truly I tell you, before the rooster crows, you will disown me three times!' The prediction is devastating. Peter's most passionate declaration of loyalty is met with the prophecy of his most shameful failure.

thenVsNow.then: Peter's boast to die for Jesus echoed the highest ideal of loyalty in Mediterranean culture. Jesus' prediction of denial was not a curse but a realistic assessment of human weakness under pressure.
thenVsNow.now: Peter's story is one of the most relatable in all of Scripture. The gap between our best intentions and our actual behavior under pressure is painfully familiar. Jesus predicts Peter's failure -- but also predicts his restoration (you will follow later).

explainer.summary: Jesus comforts his troubled disciples with some of the most beloved words in Scripture: 'In my Father's house are many rooms.' He declares himself the way, the truth, and the life -- the exclusive path to the Father. He promises that the Holy Spirit (the Paraclete/Advocate/Helper) will come after his departure to teach, remind, and guide them. He promises peace unlike anything the world offers and tells them that his departure is actually for their benefit. The chapter is pastoral theology at its finest -- Jesus preparing frightened followers for the most disorienting experience of their lives.
themes: The Father's house: many rooms
I AM the way, the truth, and the life
The unity of Father and Son
The promise of the Holy Spirit (Paraclete)
Peace that the world cannot give
Obedience as the expression of love
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 14 is the core of the Farewell Discourse. The disciples are confused, grieving, and afraid. Jesus addresses their fear with a series of promises: a prepared place, a revealed way, the Spirit's presence, and his own return. Every major theme of the discourse is introduced here and developed in the chapters that follow.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The sixth 'I AM' statement
wordStudies.meaning: The sixth 'I AM' statement. In the ancient world, 'the way' was a common metaphor for a way of life or spiritual path. Jesus claims to be not one way among many but the exclusive means of access to the Father. He is the road, not merely a signpost on the road.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Variously translated as 'Advocate,' 'Helper,' 'Com
wordStudies.meaning: Variously translated as 'Advocate,' 'Helper,' 'Comforter,' or 'Counselor.' The word literally means 'one called alongside.' In legal contexts, it referred to a defense attorney or advocate. Jesus promises 'another Paraclete' (14:16), implying that he himself has been the first. The Spirit will continue Jesus' presence after his departure.

verses.text: “Don’t let your heart be troubled. Believe in God. Believe also in me.
verses.explainer: Do not let your hearts be troubled. You believe in God; believe also in me. The opening command responds to the disciples' fear and confusion. Their teacher is leaving, their friend is about to die, and their world is collapsing. Jesus says the antidote to fear is trust.

thenVsNow.then: The disciples faced the collapse of everything they had built their lives around for three years. Jesus' departure threatened not just grief but complete disorientation -- they had left everything to follow him.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is one of the most widely quoted in times of crisis, loss, and fear. The command is not to suppress emotion but to redirect trust: when the heart is troubled, anchor it in the person of Jesus.

verses.text: In my Father’s house are many homes. If it weren’t so, I would have told you. I am going to prepare a place for you.
verses.explainer: My Father's house has many rooms; if that were not so, would I have told you that I am going there to prepare a place for you? The image is of a spacious family home with room for everyone. There is no scarcity in God's house -- space has been prepared, and it is personal.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "My Father's house has many rooms", 'explanation': "The image draws on the practice of extended families living in compounds where new rooms were added as the family grew. When a son married, a new room was added to the family dwelling. Jesus says he is going to prepare the family home for their arrival -- they are being adopted into God's household."}

verses.text: If I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again, and will receive you to myself; that where I am, you may be there also.
verses.explainer: And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come back and take you to be with me that you also may be where I am. The promise is personal presence -- 'where I am, you will be.' The ultimate hope is not a location but a relationship.

verses.text: Where I go, you know, and you know the way.”
verses.explainer: You know the way to the place where I am going. Jesus assumes they already know the way because they know him. But the disciples are not as clear on this as Jesus suggests.

verses.text: Thomas said to him, “Lord, we don’t know where you are going. How can we know the way?”
verses.explainer: Thomas says to him: 'Lord, we don't know where you are going, so how can we know the way?' Thomas voices what everyone is thinking. His honesty about confusion is a gift -- it draws out one of the most important declarations in the Gospel.

verses.text:
Jesus said to him,
“I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father, except through me.
verses.explainer: Jesus answers: 'I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.' The sixth 'I AM' statement is the most comprehensive and the most controversial. Jesus claims to be the exclusive path to God -- not a way but the way, not a truth but the truth, not a life but the life.

thenVsNow.then: In a world of competing religious claims, Jesus' exclusive claim was provocative. The Roman world valued religious tolerance and syncretism. Claiming to be the only way was socially dangerous.
thenVsNow.now: This verse remains one of the most debated in Christianity. Some see it as intolerant exclusivism; others as honest truth-telling. Jesus frames it not as a restriction but as a revelation -- he shows the way rather than blocking alternatives.

verses.text: If you had known me, you would have known my Father also. From now on, you know him, and have seen him.”
verses.explainer: If you really know me, you will know my Father as well. From now on, you do know him and have seen him. Knowing Jesus is knowing God. The long search for God ends in the person standing before them.

verses.text: Philip said to him, “Lord, show us the Father, and that will be enough for us.”
verses.explainer: Philip says: 'Lord, show us the Father and that will be enough for us.' Philip's request echoes the deepest human longing -- to see God directly. Moses asked for the same thing on Sinai.

verses.text:
Jesus said to him,
“Have I been with you such a long time, and do you not know me, Philip? He who has seen me has seen the Father. How do you say, ‘Show us the Father?’
verses.explainer: Jesus answers: 'Don't you know me, Philip, even after I have been among you such a long time? Anyone who has seen me has seen the Father. How can you say, Show me the Father?' There is gentle exasperation here. Philip has been looking at the Father for three years without realizing it. Jesus is the visible image of the invisible God.

verses.text: Don’t you believe that I am in the Father, and the Father in me? The words that I tell you, I speak not from myself; but the Father who lives in me does his works.
verses.explainer: Don't you believe that I am in the Father, and that the Father is in me? The words I say to you I do not speak on my own authority. Rather, it is the Father, living in me, who is doing his work. The mutual indwelling of Father and Son -- Jesus in the Father and the Father in Jesus -- is the theological core of John's Gospel.

verses.text: Believe me that I am in the Father, and the Father in me; or else believe me for the very works’ sake.
verses.explainer: Believe me when I say that I am in the Father and the Father is in me; or at least believe on the evidence of the works themselves. Jesus offers two paths to faith: trust his word, or examine the evidence of his miraculous works. He meets people where they are.

verses.text: Most certainly I tell you, he who believes in me, the works that I do, he will do also; and he will do greater works than these, because I am going to my Father.
verses.explainer: Very truly I tell you, whoever believes in me will do the works I have been doing, and they will do even greater things than these, because I am going to the Father. The promise is breathtaking: believers will do greater works than Jesus because his departure enables the Spirit's coming, which empowers global mission.

thenVsNow.then: The 'greater works' are not more spectacular miracles but a wider scope of impact. Jesus' earthly ministry was limited to Palestine over three years. Through the Spirit, his followers will carry the message to the entire world.
thenVsNow.now: The book of Acts records the fulfillment: thousands converted at Pentecost, the gospel spreading to every nation. The 'greater works' are still happening wherever the message reaches new people.

verses.text: Whatever you will ask in my name, that will I do, that the Father may be glorified in the Son.
verses.explainer: And I will do whatever you ask in my name, so that the Father may be glorified in the Son. Prayer 'in my name' is not a magic formula but prayer aligned with Jesus' character and purposes.

verses.text: If you will ask anything in my name, I will do it.
verses.explainer: You may ask me for anything in my name, and I will do it. The promise is conditional on the 'in my name' qualifier -- prayers that reflect Jesus' will and purpose.

verses.text: If you love me, keep my commandments.
verses.explainer: If you love me, keep my commands. Love is not sentiment but obedience. The test of genuine affection for Jesus is doing what he says.

verses.text:
I will pray to the Father, and he will give you another Counselor,

that he may be with you forever,—
verses.explainer: And I will ask the Father, and he will give you another advocate to help you and be with you forever. The word 'another' (allon) means 'another of the same kind.' The Spirit is another Jesus -- the same kind of helper, but now permanently present.

verses.text: the Spirit of truth, whom the world can’t receive; for it doesn’t see him, neither knows him. You know him, for he lives with you, and will be in you.
verses.explainer: The Spirit of truth. The world cannot accept him, because it neither sees him nor knows him. But you know him, for he lives with you and will be in you. The Spirit's presence moves from external ('with you' -- as Jesus has been) to internal ('in you' -- the new reality after Pentecost).

verses.text: I will not leave you orphans. I will come to you.
verses.explainer: I will not leave you as orphans; I will come to you. The image of orphans captures the disciples' fear -- they are about to be left alone in a hostile world. Jesus promises his return, both through the Spirit and ultimately in person.

verses.text: Yet a little while, and the world will see me no more; but you will see me. Because I live, you will live also.
verses.explainer: Before long, the world will not see me anymore, but you will see me. Because I live, you also will live. Jesus' life guarantees their life. His resurrection is the foundation of their survival.

verses.text: In that day you will know that I am in my Father, and you in me, and I in you.
verses.explainer: On that day you will realize that I am in my Father, and you are in me, and I am in you. The chain of mutual indwelling extends: Father in Son, Son in believers, believers in Son. The relationship is not distant but intimately interpenetrating.

verses.text: One who has my commandments, and keeps them, that person is one who loves me. One who loves me will be loved by my Father, and I will love him, and will reveal myself to him.”
verses.explainer: Whoever has my commands and keeps them is the one who loves me. The one who loves me will be loved by my Father, and I too will love them and show myself to them. Obedience, love, and revelation form a cycle: obeying leads to love, love leads to deeper revelation.

verses.text: Judas (not Iscariot) said to him, “Lord, what has happened that you are about to reveal yourself to us, and not to the world?”
verses.explainer: Then Judas (not Iscariot) asks: 'But, Lord, why do you intend to show yourself to us and not to the world?' This other Judas voices the natural question: why private revelation instead of public proof?

verses.text:
Jesus answered him,
“If a man loves me, he will keep my word. My Father will love him, and we will come to him, and make our home with him.
verses.explainer: Jesus replies: 'Anyone who loves me will obey my teaching. My Father will love them, and we will come to them and make our home with them.' The Father and Son take up residence in the obedient believer. The temple is no longer a building but a person.

verses.text: He who doesn’t love me doesn’t keep my words. The word which you hear isn’t mine, but the Father’s who sent me.
verses.explainer: Anyone who does not love me will not obey my teaching. These words you hear are not my own; they belong to the Father who sent me. Disobedience reveals lack of love. The standard is not arbitrary -- it comes from the Father himself.

verses.text: I have said these things to you, while still living with you.
verses.explainer: All this I have spoken while still with you. Jesus acknowledges the limitations of present teaching -- there is more to say, but the Spirit will complete the curriculum.

verses.text: But the Counselor, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name, he will teach you all things, and will remind you of all that I said to you.
verses.explainer: But the Advocate, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name, will teach you all things and will remind you of everything I have said to you. The Spirit's two primary functions are teaching and reminding. He brings new understanding and recalls Jesus' words. This verse is foundational for understanding how the Gospels were produced -- the Spirit enabled the disciples to remember and understand.

thenVsNow.then: The promise that the Spirit would remind them of Jesus' words addresses a practical concern: how would they remember everything he taught? The Spirit's role in preserving and interpreting Jesus' teaching is the basis for the authority of apostolic witness.
thenVsNow.now: This verse undergirds the Christian confidence that the New Testament writings faithfully transmit Jesus' teaching. The Spirit's work of teaching and reminding connects the historical Jesus to the written Gospels.

verses.text: Peace I leave with you. My peace I give to you; not as the world gives, give I to you. Don’t let your heart be troubled, neither let it be fearful.
verses.explainer: Peace I leave with you; my peace I give you. I do not give to you as the world gives. Do not let your hearts be troubled and do not be afraid. The peace Jesus offers is not the absence of conflict but the presence of God in the midst of conflict. The world's peace depends on circumstances; Jesus' peace transcends them.

thenVsNow.then: The Roman world boasted of the Pax Romana -- peace through military dominance. Jesus offers a radically different peace: not through conquest but through trust in God's sovereignty, even in the face of death.
thenVsNow.now: In a world of constant anxiety, this verse promises a peace that does not depend on external conditions. It is perhaps the most comforting verse in the Farewell Discourse -- a peace that survives the worst circumstances.

verses.text: You heard how I told you, ‘I go away, and I come to you.’ If you loved me, you would have rejoiced, because I said ‘I am going to my Father;’ for the Father is greater than I.
verses.explainer: You heard me say, 'I am going away and I am coming back to you.' If you loved me, you would be glad that I am going to the Father, for the Father is greater than I. Jesus says his departure should cause joy, not grief, because he is going to the Father. The phrase 'the Father is greater than I' has been much debated in Christian theology.

verses.text: Now I have told you before it happens so that, when it happens, you may believe.
verses.explainer: I have told you now before it happens, so that when it does happen you will believe. Predictive teaching serves faith -- when events unfold as Jesus said, the disciples' faith will be strengthened.

verses.text: I will no more speak much with you, for the prince of the world comes, and he has nothing in me.
verses.explainer: I will not say much more to you, for the prince of this world is coming. He has no hold over me. Satan is approaching through Judas and the arresting party. But he has no claim on Jesus -- there is no sin, no vulnerability, no leverage point.

verses.text: But that the world may know that I love the Father, and as the Father commanded me, even so I do. Arise, let us go from here.
verses.explainer: But he comes so that the world may learn that I love the Father and do exactly what my Father has commanded me. Come now; let us leave. The cross is Jesus' supreme act of obedience to the Father and his ultimate demonstration of love. The command to leave signals the transition to the next phase of teaching.

explainer.summary: Jesus uses the metaphor of the vine and branches to teach the essential nature of abiding in him. Fruitfulness depends entirely on connection to the vine; apart from Jesus, the disciples can do nothing. He elevates them from servants to friends, repeats the command to love one another, and warns that the world will hate them as it hated him. He promises the Spirit of truth will come to testify about him, and they too will testify.
themes: I AM the true vine (seventh I AM statement)
Abiding and fruitfulness
From servants to friends
The command to love one another
The world's hatred of Jesus' followers
The coming Spirit of truth
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter continues the Farewell Discourse, likely spoken as Jesus and the disciples walk toward Gethsemane. The vine metaphor is the seventh and final 'I AM' statement, completing the set. The shift from vine imagery to warnings about persecution prepares the disciples for the hostility they will face after Jesus' departure.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The key word of this chapter, appearing eleven tim
wordStudies.meaning: The key word of this chapter, appearing eleven times. It means to remain, stay, dwell, continue. In John's theology, 'abiding' describes the ongoing, intimate connection between believers and Jesus. It is not a one-time decision but a continuous state of dependence, like a branch drawing life from a vine.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The seventh and final 'I AM' statement
wordStudies.meaning: The seventh and final 'I AM' statement. Israel was frequently described as God's vine or vineyard in the Old Testament (Isaiah 5, Psalm 80, Ezekiel 15). Jesus claims to be the 'true' vine -- the reality that Israel's vine pointed toward. He is the authentic source of life for God's people.

verses.text: “I am the true vine, and my Father is the farmer.
verses.explainer: I am the true vine, and my Father is the gardener. The seventh and final 'I AM' statement. Israel was God's vine, but it repeatedly failed to produce fruit. Jesus is the true (genuine, authentic) vine that Israel was always meant to be. The Father is the vintner who tends the vineyard.

verses.text: Every branch in me that doesn’t bear fruit, he takes away. Every branch that bears fruit, he prunes, that it may bear more fruit.
verses.explainer: He cuts off every branch in me that bears no fruit, while every branch that does bear fruit he prunes so that it will be even more fruitful. The Father's care involves both removal and pruning. Pruning is painful but purposeful -- it is not punishment but cultivation.

thenVsNow.then: In the vineyards of Palestine, pruning was essential. Unpruned vines produced abundant leaves but little fruit. The vintner cut away the excessive growth so the vine's energy would go into producing grapes rather than foliage.
thenVsNow.now: The metaphor of pruning speaks to every experience of loss, difficulty, or discipline that ultimately produces growth. God's work in the believer's life includes cutting away what is unfruitful, even when it is painful.

verses.text: You are already pruned clean because of the word which I have spoken to you.
verses.explainer: You are already clean because of the word I have spoken to you. The word 'clean' (katharos) sounds like the Greek for 'pruned' (kathairō) -- a wordplay connecting spiritual cleansing with vineyard cultivation.

verses.text: Remain in me, and I in you. As the branch can’t bear fruit by itself, unless it remains in the vine, so neither can you, unless you remain in me.
verses.explainer: Remain in me, as I also remain in you. No branch can bear fruit by itself; it must remain in the vine. Neither can you bear fruit unless you remain in me. The command is mutual abiding. A branch severed from the vine dies immediately. The point is absolute dependence.

verses.text: I am the vine. You are the branches. He who remains in me, and I in him, the same bears much fruit, for apart from me you can do nothing.
verses.explainer: I am the vine; you are the branches. If you remain in me and I in you, you will bear much fruit; apart from me you can do nothing. 'Nothing' is absolute. Not 'very little' or 'somewhat less' but nothing. Every spiritual fruit flows from connection to Jesus.

thenVsNow.then: For disciples who had been empowered to perform miracles and cast out demons, the claim that they could do 'nothing' apart from Jesus was humbling. Their power had never been their own.
thenVsNow.now: This verse challenges every model of spiritual self-sufficiency. The Christian life is not a self-improvement project but a relationship of ongoing dependence. The moment the branch takes credit for its own fruit, it has forgotten the vine.

verses.text: If a man doesn’t remain in me, he is thrown out as a branch, and is withered; and they gather them, throw them into the fire, and they are burned.
verses.explainer: If you do not remain in me, you are like a branch that is thrown away and withers; such branches are picked up, thrown into the fire and burned. The imagery is sobering. Disconnection from Jesus leads to spiritual death. The fire represents judgment on fruitlessness.

verses.text: If you remain in me, and my words remain in you, you will ask whatever you desire, and it will be done for you.
verses.explainer: If you remain in me and my words remain in you, ask whatever you wish, and it will be done for you. Effective prayer flows from abiding. When Jesus' words shape the believer's desires, prayers align with God's will and are answered.

verses.text: “In this is my Father glorified, that you bear much fruit; and so you will be my disciples.
verses.explainer: This is to my Father's glory, that you bear much fruit, showing yourselves to be my disciples. Fruitfulness glorifies the Father and proves discipleship. The evidence of connection to Jesus is visible in transformed lives.

verses.text: Even as the Father has loved me, I also have loved you. Remain in my love.
verses.explainer: As the Father has loved me, so have I loved you. Now remain in my love. The quality of Jesus' love for his disciples equals the Father's love for the Son. This is the highest possible love, and they are invited to live within it.

verses.text: If you keep my commandments, you will remain in my love; even as I have kept my Father’s commandments, and remain in his love.
verses.explainer: If you keep my commands, you will remain in my love, just as I have kept my Father's commands and remain in his love. Obedience is the mechanism of abiding. Jesus models it himself -- he remains in the Father's love through obedience.

verses.text: I have spoken these things to you, that my joy may remain in you, and that your joy may be made full.
verses.explainer: I have told you this so that my joy may be in you and your joy may be complete. The purpose of the vine teaching is joy -- not grim duty but overflowing gladness that comes from fruitful connection to Jesus.

verses.text: “This is my commandment, that you love one another, even as I have loved you.
verses.explainer: My command is this: Love each other as I have loved you. The 'new commandment' from 13:34 is repeated. The standard remains the same: not human-scale love but Jesus-scale love.

verses.text: Greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends.
verses.explainer: Greater love has no one than this: to lay down one's life for one's friends. The ultimate expression of love is self-sacrifice. Jesus defines the ceiling of love as death for others -- and he is about to demonstrate it.

thenVsNow.then: In Greco-Roman culture, dying for friends was considered the highest form of honor. Soldiers who died protecting comrades were celebrated. Jesus takes this widely admired virtue and makes it the defining characteristic of his community.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is inscribed on war memorials and read at funerals around the world. It identifies the highest human love as the willingness to sacrifice everything for another person's good.

verses.text: You are my friends, if you do whatever I command you.
verses.explainer: You are my friends if you do what I command. Friendship with Jesus is conditional on obedience -- not as a transaction but as the natural expression of a genuine relationship.

verses.text: No longer do I call you servants, for the servant doesn’t know what his lord does. But I have called you friends, for everything that I heard from my Father, I have made known to you.
verses.explainer: I no longer call you servants, because a servant does not know his master's business. Instead, I have called you friends, for everything that I learned from my Father I have made known to you. The elevation from servant to friend is based on shared knowledge. Jesus has held nothing back -- he has given them full access to the Father's plan.

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient world, the distinction between a servant (doulos) and a friend (philos) of a king was immense. Servants obeyed without understanding; friends shared the king's counsel and confidence. Jesus promotes the disciples from obedient servants to trusted confidants.
thenVsNow.now: This verse transforms the believer's relationship with God from one of fearful obedience to one of intimate friendship. God does not keep his purposes secret from his people; he shares his heart.

verses.text: You didn’t choose me, but I chose you, and appointed you, that you should go and bear fruit, and that your fruit should remain; that whatever you will ask of the Father in my name, he may give it to you.
verses.explainer: You did not choose me, but I chose you and appointed you so that you might go and bear fruit -- fruit that will last -- and so that whatever you ask in my name the Father will give you. The initiative is God's, not human. They did not find Jesus; he found them. Their mission and fruitfulness flow from his choice, not their ambition.

verses.text: “I command these things to you, that you may love one another.
verses.explainer: This is my command: Love each other. For the third time in three chapters, the command to love one another is stated. The repetition makes it impossible to miss: love is not optional for those connected to the vine.

verses.text: If the world hates you, you know that it has hated me before it hated you.
verses.explainer: If the world hates you, keep in mind that it hated me first. The transition from love among believers to hatred from the world is abrupt but realistic. Connection to Jesus brings both the deepest fellowship and the fiercest opposition.

verses.text: If you were of the world, the world would love its own. But because you are not of the world, since I chose you out of the world, therefore the world hates you.
verses.explainer: If you belonged to the world, it would love you as its own. As it is, you do not belong to the world, but I have chosen you out of the world. That is why the world hates you. Hatred is not a sign of failure but of alignment. The world rejects what does not belong to it.

thenVsNow.then: By the time John wrote, Christians were experiencing increasing social exclusion and persecution. Jesus' words validated their suffering -- it was not a sign that something had gone wrong but confirmation that they belonged to Jesus rather than to the world system.
thenVsNow.now: The promise of the world's hostility challenges Christian communities that prioritize cultural acceptance. Jesus warns that authentic discipleship may provoke opposition. Comfort with the world may indicate conformity rather than faithfulness.

verses.text:
