Remember the word that I said to you: ‘A servant is not greater than his lord.’
If they persecuted me, they will also persecute you. If they kept my word, they will also keep yours.
verses.explainer: Remember what I told you: 'A servant is not greater than his master.' If they persecuted me, they will persecute you also. If they obeyed my teaching, they will obey yours also. The servants' experience mirrors the master's. If Jesus was rejected, his followers should expect the same.

verses.text: But all these things will they do to you for my name’s sake, because they don’t know him who sent me.
verses.explainer: They will treat you this way because of my name, for they do not know the one who sent me. The persecution is not personal but christological -- they are hated because of Jesus, not because of their own failures.

verses.text: If I had not come and spoken to them, they would not have had sin; but now they have no excuse for their sin.
verses.explainer: If I had not come and spoken to them, they would not be guilty of sin. As it is, they have no excuse for their sin. Jesus' coming increases accountability. Ignorance could have been an excuse; but after seeing and hearing Jesus, rejection is inexcusable.

verses.text: He who hates me, hates my Father also.
verses.explainer: Whoever hates me hates my Father as well. The inseparability of Father and Son means that the world's rejection of Jesus is rejection of God.

verses.text: If I hadn’t done among them the works which no one else did, they wouldn’t have had sin. But now have they seen and also hated both me and my Father.
verses.explainer: If I had not done among them the works no one else did, they would not be guilty of sin. As it is, they have seen, and yet they have hated both me and my Father. The unprecedented nature of Jesus' signs makes the rejection all the more culpable. They saw what no one else in history had seen, and still they hated.

verses.text: But this happened so that the word may be fulfilled which was written in their law, ‘They hated me without a cause.’
verses.explainer: But this is to fulfill what is written in their Law: 'They hated me without reason.' Even the irrational hatred against Jesus fulfills Scripture.

verses.text:
“When the Counselor
has come, whom I will send to you from the Father, the Spirit of truth, who proceeds from the Father, he will testify about me.
verses.explainer: When the Advocate comes, whom I will send to you from the Father -- the Spirit of truth who goes out from the Father -- he will testify about me. The second Paraclete passage. The Spirit's primary work is to testify about Jesus -- to make him known, real, and present.

verses.text: You will also testify, because you have been with me from the beginning.
verses.explainer: And you also must testify, for you have been with me from the beginning. The Spirit's testimony and the disciples' testimony work together. The Spirit empowers; the disciples speak. Human witness, enabled by divine power, is the mechanism by which the world hears about Jesus.

explainer.summary: Jesus continues preparing the disciples for his departure. He warns that they will be expelled from synagogues and even killed by people who think they are serving God. He teaches that the Holy Spirit will convict the world of sin, righteousness, and judgment, and will guide the disciples into all truth. Using the metaphor of a woman in labor, Jesus promises that their grief will turn to joy. He declares that he has overcome the world -- a statement of victory spoken on the eve of apparent defeat.
themes: Persecution for faith
The Spirit's convicting work in the world
The Spirit guiding into all truth
Sorrow turned to joy (the labor metaphor)
Speaking plainly about the Father
Jesus has overcome the world
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 16 concludes the Farewell Discourse proper (the High Priestly Prayer in chapter 17 is a separate form). The themes of the Spirit's work, the world's hostility, and the disciples' grief are brought to resolution. Jesus' final declaration -- 'I have overcome the world' -- is the climax of the discourse, spoken with the cross only hours away.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The Spirit's work is to 'convict' or 'expose' the
wordStudies.meaning: The Spirit's work is to 'convict' or 'expose' the world regarding sin, righteousness, and judgment. The word carries legal overtones -- like a prosecutor presenting evidence that proves guilt. The Spirit does not merely accuse; he demonstrates the truth in a way that leaves no room for denial.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Jesus uses this phrase repeatedly, creating confus
wordStudies.meaning: Jesus uses this phrase repeatedly, creating confusion among the disciples. It refers to the brief period between his death and resurrection -- a 'little while' of absence that will feel like an eternity. The phrase captures the telescoping of time in moments of grief.

verses.text: “I have said these things to you so that you wouldn’t be caused to stumble.
verses.explainer: All this I have told you so that you will not fall away. The warnings about persecution serve a pastoral purpose: forewarned is forearmed. Jesus does not want them surprised by suffering.

verses.text: They will put you out of the synagogues. Yes, the time comes that whoever kills you will think that he offers service to God.
verses.explainer: They will put you out of the synagogue; in fact, the time is coming when anyone who kills you will think they are offering a service to God. Religious persecution is the cruelest kind -- the persecutor believes he is doing God's will while destroying God's people.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Expelled from the synagogue', 'explanation': "By the time John wrote (c. 85-95 AD), Jewish Christians were being formally excluded from synagogue worship. The Birkat ha-Minim, a curse on heretics added to the synagogue liturgy, may have targeted Jewish believers in Jesus. This was not a future threat for John's readers -- it was their present reality."}

thenVsNow.then: In first-century Judaism, synagogue exclusion meant the loss of religious community, social identity, business relationships, and family connections. It was comprehensive social death. Being killed by zealous Jews who considered it service to God was a real possibility.
thenVsNow.now: Religious persecution continues globally. The dynamic Jesus describes -- sincere believers inflicting violence in God's name -- has repeated itself throughout history. Jesus' warning applies wherever religious certainty becomes violent certainty.

verses.text:
They will do these things
because they have not known the Father, nor me.
verses.explainer: They will do such things because they have not known the Father or me. The root cause of persecution is ignorance of God. Those who truly know God cannot kill in his name, because God has revealed himself as self-sacrificing love, not coercive power.

verses.text: But I have told you these things, so that when the time comes, you may remember that I told you about them. I didn’t tell you these things from the beginning, because I was with you.
verses.explainer: I have told you this so that when their time comes you will remember that I warned you about them. I did not tell you this from the beginning because I was with you. While Jesus was present, he was the target. After his departure, his followers become the targets.

verses.text: But now I am going to him who sent me, and none of you asks me, ‘Where are you going?’
verses.explainer: Now I am going to him who sent me. None of you asks me, 'Where are you going?' Earlier they asked (13:36, 14:5), but now grief has overtaken curiosity.

verses.text: But because I have told you these things, sorrow has filled your heart.
verses.explainer: Rather, you are filled with grief because I have said these things. Jesus acknowledges their emotional state. They are not thinking theologically -- they are overwhelmed by the impending loss.

verses.text: Nevertheless I tell you the truth: It is to your advantage that I go away, for if I don’t go away, the Counselor won’t come to you. But if I go, I will send him to you.
verses.explainer: But very truly I tell you, it is for your good that I am going away. Unless I go away, the Advocate will not come to you; but if I go, I will send him to you. This is one of the most counterintuitive statements in the Gospel: Jesus' physical absence is better for the disciples than his physical presence. The Spirit's universal, interior presence surpasses the limitation of Jesus' bodily location.

thenVsNow.then: The disciples could not imagine anything better than Jesus' physical company. They had walked with him, eaten with him, watched him perform miracles. The idea that his absence could be an upgrade was incomprehensible.
thenVsNow.now: This verse addresses the longing many Christians feel to have been present during Jesus' earthly ministry. Jesus says the current arrangement -- his presence through the Spirit -- is actually superior to physical proximity.

verses.text: When he has come, he will convict the world about sin, about righteousness, and about judgment;
verses.explainer: When he comes, he will prove the world to be in the wrong about sin and righteousness and judgment. The Spirit's work is forensic -- he puts the world on trial, exposing three fundamental errors.

verses.text: about sin, because they don’t believe in me;
verses.explainer: About sin, because people do not believe in me. The world's fundamental sin is not a catalog of moral failures but the refusal to believe in Jesus. Unbelief is the root from which all other sins grow.

verses.text: about righteousness, because I am going to my Father, and you won’t see me any more;
verses.explainer: About righteousness, because I am going to the Father, where you can see me no longer. Jesus' return to the Father vindicates him as righteous. The world judged him a criminal; the resurrection proves the world wrong.

verses.text: about judgment, because the prince of this world has been judged.
verses.explainer: And about judgment, because the prince of this world now stands condemned. Satan, the ruler of the world's system, has already been judged. The cross that appears to be his triumph is actually his condemnation. The Spirit makes this verdict known.

verses.text: “I have yet many things to tell you, but you can’t bear them now.
verses.explainer: I have much more to say to you, more than you can now bear. Jesus exercises pedagogical restraint -- there are truths the disciples cannot yet handle. Understanding requires the Spirit's coming and the experience of the cross and resurrection.

verses.text: However when he, the Spirit of truth, has come, he will guide you into all truth, for he will not speak from himself; but whatever he hears, he will speak. He will declare to you things that are coming.
verses.explainer: But when he, the Spirit of truth, comes, he will guide you into all the truth. He will not speak on his own; he will speak only what he hears, and he will tell you what is yet to come. The Spirit's role is interpretive, not independent. He takes what belongs to Jesus and makes it known -- illuminating, applying, and unfolding truth that was already present in Jesus' teaching.

thenVsNow.then: This promise assured the apostles that their post-resurrection understanding of Jesus would be guided by the Spirit, not just human memory. It is the theological basis for the authority of apostolic teaching.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is foundational for understanding how the New Testament came to be. The Spirit guided the apostles in remembering, understanding, and articulating what Jesus taught and meant.

verses.text: He will glorify me, for he will take from what is mine, and will declare it to you.
verses.explainer: He will glorify me because it is from me that he will receive what he will make known to you. The Spirit's work is always Christ-centered. Any spirit that draws attention to itself rather than to Jesus is not the Holy Spirit.

verses.text:
All things whatever the Father has are mine; therefore I said that he takes
of mine, and will declare it to you.
verses.explainer: All that belongs to the Father is mine. That is why I said the Spirit will receive from me what he will make known to you. The Trinitarian dynamic is fully displayed: what the Father has, the Son shares, and the Spirit communicates.

verses.text: A little while, and you will not see me. Again a little while, and you will see me.”
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'In a little while you will see me no more, and then after a little while you will see me.' The 'little while' refers to the period from crucifixion to resurrection -- roughly three days that will feel like an eternity to the grieving disciples.

verses.text:
Some of his disciples therefore said to one another, “What is this that he says to us,
‘A little while, and you won’t see me, and again a little while, and you will see me;’ and,
‘Because I go to the Father’?”
verses.explainer: At this, some of his disciples say to one another: 'What does he mean by this?' They are confused by the riddle of the 'little while.'

verses.text:
They said therefore, “What is this that he says,
‘A little while’? We don’t know what he is saying.”
verses.explainer: They keep asking: 'What does he mean by a little while? We don't understand what he is saying.' The repetition of their confusion underscores how disorienting Jesus' departure predictions are.

verses.text:
Therefore Jesus perceived that they wanted to ask him, and he said to them,
“Do you inquire among yourselves concerning this, that I said, ‘A little while, and you won’t see me, and again a little while, and you will see me?’
verses.explainer: Jesus sees that they want to ask him about this, so he says to them: 'Are you asking one another what I meant?' He perceives their unspoken questions and addresses them directly.

verses.text: Most certainly I tell you, that you will weep and lament, but the world will rejoice. You will be sorrowful, but your sorrow will be turned into joy.
verses.explainer: Very truly I tell you, you will weep and mourn while the world rejoices. You will grieve, but your grief will turn to joy. The crucifixion will reverse the emotional states: the world will celebrate its apparent victory while the disciples weep. But the reversal is temporary -- grief will become joy.

verses.text: A woman, when she gives birth, has sorrow, because her time has come. But when she has delivered the child, she doesn’t remember the anguish any more, for the joy that a human being is born into the world.
verses.explainer: A woman giving birth to a child has pain because her time has come; but when her baby is born she forgets the anguish because of her joy that a child is born into the world. The labor metaphor transforms the meaning of suffering: the pain is not pointless but productive. Something new is being born through the agony of the cross.

verses.text: Therefore you now have sorrow, but I will see you again, and your heart will rejoice, and no one will take your joy away from you.
verses.explainer: So with you: Now is your time of grief, but I will see you again and you will rejoice, and no one will take away your joy. The joy of the resurrection is permanent and unassailable. It cannot be stolen because it is grounded in an irreversible event.

verses.text: “In that day you will ask me no questions. Most certainly I tell you, whatever you may ask of the Father in my name, he will give it to you.
verses.explainer: In that day you will no longer ask me anything. Very truly I tell you, my Father will give you whatever you ask in my name. After the resurrection, the disciples will have direct access to the Father through Jesus' name. The mediator has opened the way.

verses.text: Until now, you have asked nothing in my name. Ask, and you will receive, that your joy may be made full.
verses.explainer: Until now you have not asked for anything in my name. Ask and you will receive, and your joy will be complete. Prayer 'in Jesus' name' is a new reality made possible by the cross. It is not a magic formula but an authorization -- approaching God on the basis of Jesus' finished work.

verses.text: I have spoken these things to you in figures of speech. But the time is coming when I will no more speak to you in figures of speech, but will tell you plainly about the Father.
verses.explainer: Though I have been speaking figuratively, a time is coming when I will no longer use this kind of language but will tell you plainly about my Father. The Farewell Discourse has been dense with metaphor (vine, labor, little while). After the resurrection, understanding will become direct.

verses.text: In that day you will ask in my name; and I don’t say to you, that I will pray to the Father for you,
verses.explainer: In that day you will ask in my name. I am not saying that I will ask the Father on your behalf. Jesus clarifies that he is not a separate intercessor standing between them and a reluctant God.

verses.text: for the Father himself loves you, because you have loved me, and have believed that I came from God.
verses.explainer: No, the Father himself loves you because you have loved me and have believed that I came from God. The Father's love is direct and personal. Believing in Jesus opens a direct relationship with the Father -- no additional mediator needed beyond what Jesus has already accomplished.

verses.text: I came from the Father, and have come into the world. Again, I leave the world, and go to the Father.”
verses.explainer: I came from the Father and entered the world; now I am leaving the world and going back to the Father. The entire Gospel compressed into one sentence: origin (from the Father), incarnation (entered the world), departure (leaving the world), and return (going to the Father).

verses.text: His disciples said to him, “Behold, now you are speaking plainly, and using no figures of speech.
verses.explainer: Then Jesus' disciples say: 'Now you are speaking clearly and without figures of speech.' The disciples think they have finally understood.

verses.text: Now we know that you know all things, and don’t need for anyone to question you. By this we believe that you came from God.”
verses.explainer: Now we can see that you know all things and that you do not even need to have anyone ask you questions. This makes us believe that you came from God. Their confidence is genuine but premature.

verses.text:
Jesus answered them,
“Do you now believe?
verses.explainer: Jesus responds: 'Do you now believe?' The question carries gentle irony. Their faith, real as it is, is about to be severely tested.

verses.text: Behold, the time is coming, yes, and has now come, that you will be scattered, everyone to his own place, and you will leave me alone. Yet I am not alone, because the Father is with me.
verses.explainer: A time is coming and in fact has come when you will be scattered, each to your own home. You will leave me all alone. Yet I am not alone, for my Father is with me. Jesus predicts their desertion with compassion, not condemnation. When every human support fails, the Father remains.

verses.text: I have told you these things, that in me you may have peace. In the world you have oppression; but cheer up! I have overcome the world.”
verses.explainer: I have told you these things, so that in me you may have peace. In this world you will have trouble. But take heart! I have overcome the world. The final sentence of the Farewell Discourse proper is a declaration of victory. Jesus speaks of overcoming in the past tense -- the battle is already won, even though the cross is still ahead. The world's worst (trouble, persecution, death) has already been defeated by Jesus.

thenVsNow.then: Jesus declares victory while standing on the threshold of arrest, torture, and execution. By any human measure, he is about to lose. But in John's theology, the cross IS the victory -- it is where evil exhausts itself against self-giving love and is overcome.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is one of the most frequently quoted in the New Testament. 'I have overcome the world' is not a denial of suffering but a declaration that suffering does not have the last word. Peace is possible not because trouble is absent but because Jesus has already won.

explainer.summary: Chapter 17 is the longest recorded prayer of Jesus -- often called the 'High Priestly Prayer' because Jesus intercedes for his people like a priest before the altar. He prays first for himself (glorification through the cross), then for his disciples (protection and sanctification), then for all future believers (unity). The prayer reveals the inner life of the Trinity -- the mutual love and glory shared between Father and Son -- and extends that love to encompass all who will ever believe. It is the most intimate passage in the Gospels, a window into the heart of God.
themes: Jesus prays for his own glorification
Eternal life defined as knowing God
Protection for the disciples in a hostile world
Sanctification through truth
Unity of all believers as a witness to the world
The love between Father and Son extended to believers
explainer.contextInBook: The High Priestly Prayer is the climax of the Farewell Discourse and the theological summit of the Gospel. Everything John has been building toward converges here: the mutual indwelling of Father and Son, the mission of the disciples, the coming of the Spirit, the unity of the church, and the ultimate destiny of believers. After this prayer, Jesus crosses the Kidron Valley to Gethsemane and his arrest.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The word appears eight times in this prayer
wordStudies.meaning: The word appears eight times in this prayer. For Jesus, glory is not human fame but the full revelation of God's nature. The cross is the hour of glorification because it is where God's self-giving love is most fully displayed. Father and Son glorify each other through the mutual revelation of their shared character.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To set apart for sacred use
wordStudies.meaning: To set apart for sacred use. Jesus asks the Father to sanctify the disciples 'by the truth' -- to make them holy through the truth of his word. He also says he sanctifies himself for their sake, referring to his self-offering on the cross. The disciples are set apart for mission just as Jesus was set apart for sacrifice.

verses.text:
Jesus said these things, and lifting up his eyes to heaven, he said,
“Father, the time has come. Glorify your Son, that your Son may also glorify you;
verses.explainer: After Jesus says this, he looks toward heaven and prays: 'Father, the hour has come. Glorify your Son, that your Son may glorify you.' The prayer opens with the word 'Father' -- the most intimate address possible. The hour that has been approaching throughout the Gospel has finally arrived. Jesus asks for glorification not for his own sake but so that the Father may be glorified through him.

thenVsNow.then: Jewish prayer was typically offered standing with arms raised and eyes lifted toward heaven. Jesus adopts this posture. The prayer is spoken aloud so the disciples can hear it -- they are meant to overhear this intimate conversation between Father and Son.
thenVsNow.now: The High Priestly Prayer has been called 'the holy of holies of the New Testament.' Listening to Jesus pray for his followers is like overhearing a conversation you were never meant to hear -- except that Jesus intends for every believer to know what he prays on their behalf.

verses.text: even as you gave him authority over all flesh, so he will give eternal life to all whom you have given him.
verses.explainer: For you granted him authority over all people that he might give eternal life to all those you have given him. Jesus' authority is for one purpose: giving eternal life. Power exists for the sake of love.

verses.text: This is eternal life, that they should know you, the only true God, and him whom you sent, Jesus Christ.
verses.explainer: Now this is eternal life: that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom you have sent. The only definition of eternal life in the Bible. It is not primarily about duration (living forever) but about relationship (knowing God and Jesus). Eternal life is a quality of existence, not just a quantity of time.

thenVsNow.then: In Jewish thought, 'knowing' God meant far more than intellectual awareness. It meant intimate, experiential relationship -- the kind of knowing that transforms the knower. The Hebrew word for 'know' (yada) carried connotations of the deepest personal intimacy.
thenVsNow.now: This definition reframes eternal life for every generation. It is not a reward for the afterlife but a relationship that begins now. To 'know' God is to experience his love, character, and purposes in a transforming personal encounter.

verses.text: I glorified you on the earth. I have accomplished the work which you have given me to do.
verses.explainer: I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do. Jesus speaks of his work as already completed, even before the cross. In God's economy, the decision is as good as the deed. The work was finishing before the finishing blow.

verses.text: Now, Father, glorify me with your own self with the glory which I had with you before the world existed.
verses.explainer: And now, Father, glorify me in your presence with the glory I had with you before the world began. Jesus asks to return to the pre-incarnate glory he shared with the Father before creation. This is not a request for new honor but for the restoration of eternal glory temporarily veiled by human flesh.

verses.text: I revealed your name to the people whom you have given me out of the world. They were yours, and you have given them to me. They have kept your word.
verses.explainer: I have revealed you to those whom you gave me out of the world. They were yours; you gave them to me and they have obeyed your word. Jesus' ministry has been fundamentally revelatory -- making the invisible Father visible. The disciples belong to the Father first and are given to the Son.

verses.text: Now they have known that all things whatever you have given me are from you,
verses.explainer: Now they know that everything you have given me comes from you. The disciples have grasped the essential truth: Jesus' authority, teaching, and mission all originate in the Father.

verses.text: for the words which you have given me I have given to them, and they received them, and knew for sure that I came from you, and they have believed that you sent me.
verses.explainer: For I gave them the words you gave me and they accepted them. They knew with certainty that I came from you, and they believed that you sent me. The chain of transmission is complete: Father to Son to disciples. Their faith, though imperfect, is real.

verses.text: I pray for them. I don’t pray for the world, but for those whom you have given me, for they are yours.
verses.explainer: I pray for them. I am not praying for the world, but for those you have given me, for they are yours. Jesus prays specifically for his disciples, not the world at large. This is not a dismissal of the world (he will expand the prayer in v. 20) but a focused intercession for those who will carry the mission forward.

verses.text: All things that are mine are yours, and yours are mine, and I am glorified in them.
verses.explainer: All I have is yours, and all you have is mine. And glory has come to me through them. The mutual sharing between Father and Son extends to a mutual sharing in the disciples. Jesus finds glory in his followers -- their faith reflects his character.

verses.text: I am no more in the world, but these are in the world, and I am coming to you. Holy Father, keep them through your name which you have given me, that they may be one, even as we are.
verses.explainer: I will remain in the world no longer, but they are still in the world, and I am coming to you. Holy Father, protect them by the power of your name, the name you gave me, so that they may be one as we are one. The first petition: protection. The disciples will be left in a hostile world without Jesus' physical presence. He asks the Father to guard them and to preserve their unity -- a unity modeled on the unity of Father and Son.

thenVsNow.then: The prayer for protection was urgent. Jesus knew that his arrest would scatter the disciples (16:32) and that the world would persecute them (15:18-20). Without divine protection, the fledgling movement would be crushed.
thenVsNow.now: The prayer for unity remains one of the most convicting aspects of this chapter. Jesus prays that his followers would be one 'as we are one' -- a standard of unity that most Christian communities struggle to approach.

verses.text: While I was with them in the world, I kept them in your name. Those whom you have given me I have kept. None of them is lost, except the son of destruction, that the Scripture might be fulfilled.
verses.explainer: While I was with them, I protected them and kept them safe by that name you gave me. None has been lost except the one doomed to destruction so that Scripture would be fulfilled. Jesus has been their guardian. Only Judas was lost, and even that loss was foreseen in Scripture.

verses.text: But now I come to you, and I say these things in the world, that they may have my joy made full in themselves.
verses.explainer: I am coming to you now, but I say these things while I am still in the world, so that they may have the full measure of my joy within them. Jesus prays aloud so the disciples can hear and receive joy. His joy is meant to be their joy -- full, overflowing, and grounded in the Father's love.

verses.text: I have given them your word. The world hated them, because they are not of the world, even as I am not of the world.
verses.explainer: I have given them your word and the world has hated them, for they are not of the world any more than I am of the world. The disciples' possession of God's word sets them apart from the world and triggers the world's hostility.

verses.text: I pray not that you would take them from the world, but that you would keep them from the evil one.
verses.explainer: My prayer is not that you take them out of the world but that you protect them from the evil one. Jesus does not pray for extraction but for protection. The disciples must remain in the world to fulfill their mission. Escape is not the goal; preservation in the midst of danger is.

thenVsNow.then: Some Jewish groups (like the Essenes at Qumran) withdrew from the world entirely to maintain purity. Jesus explicitly rejects this model. His followers are to be in the world, engaging with it, but protected from the evil one's power.
thenVsNow.now: This verse challenges both retreat from the world and uncritical embrace of it. Jesus' model is engagement with protection -- being present in the world without being absorbed by its values.

verses.text: They are not of the world even as I am not of the world.
verses.explainer: They are not of the world, even as I am not of it. The disciples' identity is defined by their relationship to Jesus, not by their cultural context. They are in the world but not of it.

verses.text: Sanctify them in your truth. Your word is truth.
verses.explainer: Sanctify them by the truth; your word is truth. The second petition: sanctification. 'Set them apart through truth' -- God's word is the agent of holiness. Truth is not abstract but active; it transforms those who receive it.

verses.text: As you sent me into the world, even so I have sent them into the world.
verses.explainer: As you sent me into the world, I have sent them into the world. The mission of the disciples mirrors the mission of the Son. Just as the Father sent Jesus, Jesus sends his followers. The pattern is incarnational -- going into the world, not withdrawing from it.

verses.text: For their sakes I sanctify myself, that they themselves also may be sanctified in truth.
verses.explainer: For them I sanctify myself, that they too may be truly sanctified. Jesus 'sets himself apart' for sacrifice -- he consecrates himself as the offering that will make their holiness possible. His self-sacrifice is the foundation of their sanctification.

verses.text: Not for these only do I pray, but for those also who will believe in me through their word,
verses.explainer: My prayer is not for them alone. I pray also for those who will believe in me through their message. The prayer expands from the eleven to all future believers. Every Christian in history is included in this prayer. Jesus prays for people who will not be born for centuries.

thenVsNow.then: Jesus looks beyond the upper room to a future he alone can see -- millions of people across every nation and generation who will come to faith through the apostolic message. He prays for them before they exist.
thenVsNow.now: Every person who believes in Jesus through the testimony of the apostles (which includes the New Testament) is covered by this prayer. It is a staggering thought: on the night before his death, Jesus prayed specifically for you.

verses.text: that they may all be one; even as you, Father, are in me, and I in you, that they also may be one in us; that the world may believe that you sent me.
verses.explainer: That all of them may be one, Father, just as you are in me and I am in you. May they also be in us so that the world may believe that you have sent me. The third petition: unity. The unity Jesus prays for is not organizational uniformity but relational oneness modeled on the Trinity. The purpose of unity is evangelistic -- the world will believe when it sees the love between believers.

verses.text: The glory which you have given me, I have given to them; that they may be one, even as we are one;
verses.explainer: I have given them the glory that you gave me, that they may be one as we are one. The glory Jesus shares with believers is the glory of self-giving love. This shared glory creates unity because it draws everyone toward the same center.

verses.text: I in them, and you in me, that they may be perfected into one; that the world may know that you sent me, and loved them, even as you loved me.
verses.explainer: I in them and you in me -- so that they may be brought to complete unity. Then the world will know that you sent me and have loved them even as you have loved me. The chain of indwelling reaches its fullest expression: the Father in the Son, the Son in believers, producing unity that testifies to the world. The Father loves believers with the same love he has for the Son -- this is perhaps the most astonishing claim in the entire prayer.

verses.text: Father, I desire that they also whom you have given me be with me where I am, that they may see my glory, which you have given me, for you loved me before the foundation of the world.
verses.explainer: Father, I want those you have given me to be with me where I am, and to see my glory, the glory you have given me because you loved me before the creation of the world. The final request is for the ultimate destiny of believers: to be with Jesus and to see his eternal glory. The love between Father and Son predates creation. Believers are invited into that pre-cosmic love.

thenVsNow.then: The Jewish hope for the righteous was to be in God's presence after death. Jesus specifies: the destination is his own glorified presence, and the sight they will see is his eternal glory -- the glory of being loved by the Father before the universe existed.
thenVsNow.now: This verse has sustained Christian hope through centuries of persecution and suffering. The ultimate destiny is not a place but a sight -- seeing Jesus as he truly is, and being with him in the glory that existed before the stars were formed.

verses.text: Righteous Father, the world hasn’t known you, but I knew you; and these knew that you sent me.
verses.explainer: Righteous Father, though the world does not know you, I know you, and they know that you have sent me. Jesus addresses the Father as 'righteous' -- affirming that God's justice will vindicate the truth even though the world rejects it.

verses.text: I made known to them your name, and will make it known; that the love with which you loved me may be in them, and I in them.”
verses.explainer: I have made you known to them, and will continue to make you known in order that the love you have for me may be in them and that I myself may be in them. The prayer ends with the goal of all revelation: that the Father's love for the Son would dwell in believers, and that Jesus himself would be present in them. The prayer is complete. Jesus has entrusted his followers to the Father's care and committed himself to the cross.

explainer.summary: Jesus crosses the Kidron Valley to a garden where Judas leads an armed detachment to arrest him. When Jesus says 'I am he,' the soldiers fall to the ground. Peter cuts off the high priest's servant's ear. Jesus is taken first to Annas (the power behind the priestly throne), then to Caiaphas, and finally to Pilate. Peter denies Jesus three times in the courtyard. The trial before Pilate becomes a philosophical dialogue about truth and kingship, with Pilate caught between his recognition of Jesus' innocence and the political pressure of the crowd.
themes: Jesus' sovereign control over his own arrest
The falling of the soldiers at 'I AM'
Peter's three denials
The trial before Annas and Caiaphas
Pilate's examination: 'What is truth?'
The choice of Barabbas
explainer.contextInBook: The passion narrative begins. John's account differs from the Synoptics in emphasis: Jesus is not a passive victim but a sovereign figure who controls the proceedings from arrest through sentencing. He goes willingly, protects his disciples, and engages Pilate in theological dialogue. The trial before Pilate is John's most developed scene -- a dramatic staging of the confrontation between divine truth and political power.

wordStudies.translatedAs: When Jesus says 'I am he' to the arresting party,
wordStudies.meaning: When Jesus says 'I am he' to the arresting party, the Greek is simply egō eimi -- 'I AM,' the divine name. The soldiers fall backward, knocked down by the power of the name. Even in his arrest, Jesus reveals his divinity.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Jesus tells Pilate 'my kingdom is not of this worl
wordStudies.meaning: Jesus tells Pilate 'my kingdom is not of this world.' The word basileia means rule, reign, or sovereign domain. Jesus is not disclaiming kingship but redefining it -- his kingdom operates by different principles (truth, love, sacrifice) rather than the world's principles (force, coercion, violence).

verses.text: When Jesus had spoken these words, he went out with his disciples over the brook Kidron, where there was a garden, into which he and his disciples entered.
verses.explainer: When he finishes praying, Jesus leaves with his disciples and crosses the Kidron Valley. On the other side there is a garden, and he and his disciples go into it. The Kidron Valley separates Jerusalem from the Mount of Olives. John does not name the garden (Gethsemane), nor does he describe the agony of prayer recorded in the Synoptics. In John, Jesus approaches the cross with sovereign composure.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'The Kidron Valley', 'explanation': 'A deep ravine between the eastern wall of Jerusalem and the Mount of Olives. During Passover, the blood from sacrificial lambs in the Temple drained into the Kidron. Jesus, the true Passover Lamb, crosses this valley of blood on his way to his own sacrifice.'}

verses.text: Now Judas, who betrayed him, also knew the place, for Jesus often met there with his disciples.
verses.explainer: Now Judas, who betrayed him, knew the place, because Jesus had often met there with his disciples. The garden was a familiar meeting point -- Judas' betrayal exploits intimate knowledge of Jesus' habits.

verses.text: Judas then, having taken a detachment of soldiers and officers from the chief priests and the Pharisees, came there with lanterns, torches, and weapons.
verses.explainer: So Judas comes to the garden, guiding a detachment of soldiers and some officials from the chief priests and the Pharisees. They are carrying torches, lanterns and weapons. The detail about torches and lanterns is deeply ironic -- they bring artificial light to arrest the Light of the World.

verses.text:
Jesus therefore, knowing all the things that were happening to him, went out, and said to them,
“Who are you looking for?”
verses.explainer: Jesus, knowing all that is going to happen to him, goes out and asks them: 'Who is it you want?' John emphasizes Jesus' foreknowledge. He is not surprised or ambushed. He steps forward and takes the initiative.

verses.text:
They answered him, “Jesus of Nazareth.”
Jesus said to them,
“I am he.”
Judas also, who betrayed him, was standing with them.
verses.explainer: 'Jesus of Nazareth,' they reply. 'I am he,' Jesus says. (And Judas the traitor is standing there with them.) The Greek is ego eimi -- 'I AM.' On the surface, it is self-identification. At the deeper level, it is the divine name. Judas stands with the arresting party, having crossed fully to the other side.

thenVsNow.then: The 'I AM' declaration echoes God's self-revelation to Moses at the burning bush (Exodus 3:14). In the garden, the divine name is spoken by the one who bears it -- and the physical impact on the soldiers demonstrates its power.
thenVsNow.now: The scene inverts every expectation: the arrested man controls the arrest. The one being taken is the one giving himself. Jesus' use of the divine name in this moment is a declaration that even crucifixion cannot diminish his identity.

verses.text:
When therefore he said to them,
“I am he,” they went backward, and fell to the ground.
verses.explainer: When Jesus says 'I am he,' they draw back and fall to the ground. The armed detachment is knocked backward by the force of the divine name. This detail, unique to John, demonstrates that Jesus is not overpowered -- he chooses to be taken. No one takes his life; he gives it voluntarily.

verses.text:
Again therefore he asked them,
“Who are you looking for?”
They said, “Jesus of Nazareth.”
verses.explainer: Again he asks them: 'Who is it you want?' 'Jesus of Nazareth,' they say. He asks the same question a second time, calmly and deliberately, while the soldiers are recovering from the ground.

verses.text:
Jesus answered,
“I told you that I am he. If therefore you seek me, let these go their way,”
verses.explainer: Jesus answers: 'I told you that I am he. If you are looking for me, then let these men go.' Even in his arrest, Jesus protects his disciples. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep while ensuring the sheep escape.

verses.text:
that the word might be fulfilled which he spoke,
“Of those whom you have given me, I have lost none.”
verses.explainer: This happened so that the words he had spoken would be fulfilled: 'I have not lost one of those you gave me.' John connects Jesus' protective action to the prayer of chapter 17 -- physical protection of the disciples fulfills his promise to the Father.

verses.text: Simon Peter therefore, having a sword, drew it, and struck the high priest’s servant, and cut off his right ear. The servant’s name was Malchus.
verses.explainer: Then Simon Peter, who has a sword, draws it and strikes the high priest's servant, cutting off his right ear. (The servant's name is Malchus.) Peter resorts to violence -- the exact opposite of what Jesus is demonstrating. John alone names the servant (Malchus), a detail that suggests eyewitness knowledge.

verses.text:
Jesus therefore said to Peter,
“Put the sword into its sheath. The cup which the Father has given me, shall I not surely drink it?”
verses.explainer: Jesus commands Peter: 'Put your sword away! Shall I not drink the cup the Father has given me?' The 'cup' is the cup of suffering -- the Father's will that Jesus has resolved to accept. Peter's violence would frustrate the very purpose of the hour.

verses.text: So the detachment, the commanding officer, and the officers of the Jews, seized Jesus and bound him,
verses.explainer: Then the detachment of soldiers with its commander and the Jewish officials arrest Jesus. They bind him. The binding is symbolic as well as physical -- the one who came to set captives free is himself bound.

verses.text: and led him to Annas first, for he was father-in-law to Caiaphas, who was high priest that year.
verses.explainer: And they bring him first to Annas, who was the father-in-law of Caiaphas, the high priest that year. John alone records this preliminary hearing before Annas.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "Annas' continuing influence", 'explanation': "Though Rome had deposed Annas in 15 AD, he continued to wield enormous influence. Many Jews considered the high priesthood a lifetime appointment, so Annas was still regarded as the legitimate high priest. His family controlled the Temple commerce and profited enormously from it -- Jesus' cleansing of the Temple directly threatened the Annas family's income."}

verses.text: Now it was Caiaphas who advised the Jews that it was expedient that one man should perish for the people.
verses.explainer: Caiaphas was the one who had advised the Jewish leaders that it would be good if one man died for the people. John reminds the reader of Caiaphas' unwitting prophecy from 11:49-50.

verses.text: Simon Peter followed Jesus, as did another disciple. Now that disciple was known to the high priest, and entered in with Jesus into the court of the high priest;
verses.explainer: Simon Peter and another disciple follow Jesus. Because this disciple is known to the high priest, he goes with Jesus into the high priest's courtyard. The 'other disciple' is likely the beloved disciple, whose connection to the high priest's household gives him access.

verses.text: but Peter was standing at the door outside. So the other disciple, who was known to the high priest, went out and spoke to her who kept the door, and brought in Peter.
verses.explainer: But Peter has to wait outside at the door. The other disciple, who is known to the high priest, comes back, speaks to the servant girl on duty there and brings Peter in. The beloved disciple's access gets Peter through the door -- into the courtyard where his denials will occur.

verses.text:
Then the maid who kept the door said to Peter, “Are you also one of this man’s disciples?”
He said, “I am not.”
verses.explainer: The servant girl at the door asks Peter: 'You aren't one of this man's disciples too, are you?' He replies: 'I am not.' The first denial. The question comes from a young female servant -- the lowest-status person in the household. Peter, who promised to die for Jesus, buckles under the most casual interrogation.

thenVsNow.then: Peter's denial to a servant girl would have been seen as deeply shameful in honor culture. A man of his temperament -- bold, aggressive, willing to draw a sword -- folds under the mildest social pressure.
thenVsNow.now: Peter's denials remain one of the most psychologically realistic passages in the Gospels. The gap between brave promises and cowardly performance under pressure is painfully familiar to anyone who has compromised their convictions out of fear.

verses.text: Now the servants and the officers were standing there, having made a fire of coals, for it was cold. They were warming themselves. Peter was with them, standing and warming himself.
verses.explainer: It is cold, and the servants and officials stand around a fire they have made to keep warm. Peter also is standing with them, warming himself. Peter stands with Jesus' enemies, warming himself at their fire. The physical warmth contrasts with his spiritual coldness.

verses.text: The high priest therefore asked Jesus about his disciples, and about his teaching.
verses.explainer: Meanwhile, the high priest questions Jesus about his disciples and his teaching. Annas wants information about the movement's organization and doctrine.

verses.text:
Jesus answered him,
“I spoke openly to the world. I always taught in synagogues, and in the temple, where the Jews always meet. I said nothing in secret.
verses.explainer: Jesus replies: 'I have spoken openly to the world. I always taught in synagogues or at the Temple, where all the Jews come together. I said nothing in secret.' Jesus has nothing to hide. His teaching has been public from the beginning.

verses.text: Why do you ask me? Ask those who have heard me what I said to them. Behold, these know the things which I said.”
verses.explainer: Why question me? Ask those who heard me. Surely they know what I said. Jesus challenges the legality of the proceedings -- witnesses should be called, not the defendant interrogated.

verses.text: When he had said this, one of the officers standing by slapped Jesus with his hand, saying, “Do you answer the high priest like that?”
verses.explainer: When Jesus says this, one of the officials nearby slaps him in the face. 'Is this the way you answer the high priest?' he demands. The violence begins. Jesus is struck for asserting his legal rights.

verses.text:
Jesus answered him,
“If I have spoken evil, testify of the evil; but if well, why do you beat me?”
verses.explainer: Jesus replies: 'If I said something wrong, testify as to what is wrong. But if I spoke the truth, why did you strike me?' Jesus' response is measured and dignified. He does not retaliate but appeals to justice.

verses.text: Annas sent him bound to Caiaphas, the high priest.
verses.explainer: Then Annas sends him bound to Caiaphas the high priest. The preliminary hearing yields nothing. Jesus is passed to the official high priest for the formal proceeding that John does not describe in detail.

verses.text:
Now Simon Peter was standing and warming himself. They said therefore to him, “You aren’t also one of his disciples, are you?”
He denied it, and said, “I am not.”
verses.explainer: Meanwhile, Simon Peter is still standing there warming himself. So they ask him: 'You aren't one of his disciples too, are you?' He denies it, saying: 'I am not.' The second denial. The Greek egō ouk eimi ('I am not') contrasts with Jesus' egō eimi ('I AM'). While Jesus declares who he is, Peter denies who he is.

verses.text: One of the servants of the high priest, being a relative of him whose ear Peter had cut off, said, “Didn’t I see you in the garden with him?”
verses.explainer: One of the high priest's servants, a relative of the man whose ear Peter had cut off, challenges him: 'Didn't I see you with him in the garden?' The third challenge comes from a relative of Malchus -- a witness who can directly connect Peter to the arrest scene. The danger is now specific and personal.

verses.text: Peter therefore denied it again, and immediately the rooster crowed.
verses.explainer: Again Peter denies it, and at that moment a rooster crows. The third denial. Jesus' prediction (13:38) is fulfilled to the letter. John records no weeping -- the rooster's crow simply marks the moment, and Peter's anguish is left to the reader's imagination.

verses.text: They led Jesus therefore from Caiaphas into the Praetorium. It was early, and they themselves didn’t enter into the Praetorium, that they might not be defiled, but might eat the Passover.
verses.explainer: Then the Jewish leaders take Jesus from Caiaphas to the palace of the Roman governor. By now it is early morning, and to avoid ceremonial uncleanness they do not enter the palace, because they want to be able to eat the Passover. The irony is savage -- they are scrupulous about ritual purity while delivering an innocent man to execution.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Ceremonial uncleanness', 'explanation': "Entering a Gentile building rendered a Jew ritually impure, requiring purification before eating the Passover meal. The Jewish leaders carefully avoid contamination from Pilate's residence while engineering the murder of the Messiah. They strain out a gnat and swallow a camel."}

thenVsNow.then: The contrast between ritual punctiliousness and moral corruption was exactly the kind of hypocrisy that Jesus had repeatedly condemned. They will not enter a Gentile building but will demand an innocent man's blood.
thenVsNow.now: The scene remains a powerful warning against substituting ritual observance for moral integrity. Religious people are capable of meticulous attention to external rules while committing profound injustice.

verses.text: Pilate therefore went out to them, and said, “What accusation do you bring against this man?”
verses.explainer: So Pilate comes out to them and asks: 'What charges are you bringing against this man?' Pilate must accommodate their religious scruples by coming outside. The Roman governor steps out of his own palace to begin the trial.

verses.text: They answered him, “If this man weren’t an evildoer, we wouldn’t have delivered him up to you.”
verses.explainer: They reply: 'If he were not a criminal, we would not have handed him over to you.' The response is evasive -- they refuse to state a specific charge and instead demand that Pilate accept their judgment on trust.

verses.text:
Pilate therefore said to them, “Take him yourselves, and judge him according to your law.”
Therefore the Jews said to him, “It is not lawful for us to put anyone to death,”
verses.explainer: Pilate says: 'Take him yourselves and judge him by your own law.' The Jews reply: 'But we have no right to execute anyone.' The real agenda is exposed -- they want the death penalty, which only Rome can impose.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'No right to execute', 'explanation': 'Under Roman rule, the Sanhedrin had lost the power of capital punishment (jus gladii). Only the Roman governor could order execution. This is why the trial must go to Pilate. The exception was for violations of the Temple sanctuary -- a Gentile entering the inner courts could be executed by Jewish authorities.'}

verses.text: that the word of Jesus might be fulfilled, which he spoke, signifying by what kind of death he should die.
verses.explainer: This happens so that the words Jesus had spoken indicating the kind of death he was going to die would be fulfilled. John notes that Roman execution meant crucifixion, not the Jewish method of stoning. Jesus had predicted being 'lifted up' -- a death uniquely Roman.

verses.text: Pilate therefore entered again into the Praetorium, called Jesus, and said to him, “Are you the King of the Jews?”
verses.explainer: Pilate then goes back inside the palace, summons Jesus and asks him: 'Are you the king of the Jews?' The central question of the trial. From this point, Pilate moves back and forth between Jesus inside and the Jewish leaders outside -- literally caught between two worlds.

verses.text:
Jesus answered him,
“Do you say this by yourself, or did others tell you about me?”
verses.explainer: Jesus asks: 'Is that your own idea, or did others talk to you about me?' Jesus turns the interrogation around. He wants to know whether Pilate is asking politically (Are you a revolutionary?) or theologically (Are you the Messiah?).

verses.text: Pilate answered, “I’m not a Jew, am I? Your own nation and the chief priests delivered you to me. What have you done?”
verses.explainer: Pilate responds: 'Am I a Jew? Your own people and chief priests handed you over to me. What is it you have done?' Pilate distances himself from Jewish theological concerns. He wants facts, not theology.

verses.text:
Jesus answered,
“My Kingdom is not of this world. If my Kingdom were of this world, then my servants would fight, that I wouldn’t be delivered to the Jews. But now my Kingdom is not from here.”
verses.explainer: Jesus answers: 'My kingdom is not of this world. If it were, my servants would fight to prevent my arrest by the Jewish leaders. But now my kingdom is from another place.' Jesus does not deny being a king. He redefines kingship. His kingdom does not operate by the world's methods -- force, coercion, military power. Peter's sword was the wrong weapon entirely.

thenVsNow.then: Pilate understood kingdoms as territories defended by armies. Jesus describes a kingdom that comes from 'another place' -- not a geographical territory but a different order of reality entirely. Pilate has no category for this.
thenVsNow.now: This verse has been debated for centuries. Some hear Jesus saying his kingdom has nothing to do with this world (a purely spiritual kingdom). Others hear him saying his kingdom originates from beyond this world but very much operates within it. The key word is 'from' -- the kingdom's source and methods are otherworldly, even as its impact is this-worldly.

verses.text:
Pilate therefore said to him, “Are you a king then?”
Jesus answered,
“You say that I am a king. For this reason I have been born, and for this reason I have come into the world, that I should testify to the truth. Everyone who is of the truth listens to my voice.”
verses.explainer: 'You are a king, then!' says Pilate. Jesus answers: 'You say that I am a king. In fact, the reason I was born and came into the world is to testify to the truth. Everyone on the side of truth listens to me.' Jesus redefines kingship as testimony to truth. His authority is not coercive but persuasive -- those who are 'of the truth' recognize his voice.

verses.text:
Pilate said to him, “What is truth?”
When he had said this, he went out again to the Jews, and said to them, “I find no basis for a charge against him.
verses.explainer: 'What is truth?' retorts Pilate. With this he goes out again to the Jews gathered there and says: 'I find no basis for a charge against him.' Pilate's question is one of the most famous in literature. It may be cynical (truth is irrelevant to politics), philosophical (truth is unknowable), or weary (truth is a luxury a governor cannot afford). He does not wait for an answer. The irony is that Truth itself stands before him, and he walks away.

thenVsNow.then: Pilate's question reflected a widespread skepticism in the Roman world. In an empire that ruled through power, truth was whatever served the state. The idea that truth was a person standing before him in chains would have been absurd.
thenVsNow.now: Pilate's question echoes in every era that struggles with relativism and skepticism. 'What is truth?' is asked in university classrooms, political debates, and personal crises. John's answer is not a proposition but a person: truth is Jesus himself.

verses.text: But you have a custom, that I should release someone to you at the Passover. Therefore, do you want me to release to you the King of the Jews?”
verses.explainer: 'But it is your custom for me to release to you one prisoner at the time of the Passover. Do you want me to release the king of the Jews?' Pilate attempts to use a Passover amnesty to release Jesus without confronting the Jewish leaders directly.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Passover amnesty', 'explanation': "The custom of releasing a prisoner during Passover is attested in the Gospels and has some parallels in Roman provincial practice. It was a political concession designed to generate goodwill during the volatile Passover season, when Jerusalem's population swelled with nationalistic pilgrims."}

verses.text: Then they all shouted again, saying, “Not this man, but Barabbas!” Now Barabbas was a robber.
verses.explainer: They shout back: 'No, not him! Give us Barabbas!' Now Barabbas had taken part in an uprising. The crowd chooses a violent revolutionary over the Prince of Peace. Barabbas means 'son of the father' -- the false son of the father is released while the true Son of the Father is condemned.

explainer.summary: Pilate has Jesus flogged and the soldiers mock him with a crown of thorns and purple robe. Pilate presents Jesus to the crowd with the words 'Behold the man!' but the crowd demands crucifixion. Pilate resists but eventually capitulates under political pressure. Jesus carries his own cross to Golgotha and is crucified between two others. Pilate posts a sign in three languages: 'Jesus of Nazareth, the King of the Jews.' From the cross, Jesus entrusts his mother to the beloved disciple. He says 'It is finished' and gives up his spirit. His side is pierced, and blood and water flow out. Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus bury him in a new tomb.
themes: Ecce Homo: Behold the man
Pilate's capitulation to political pressure
The crucifixion and its theological significance
Jesus' care for his mother from the cross
It is finished (tetelestai)
The piercing and the blood and water
Burial by Nicodemus and Joseph
explainer.contextInBook: The crucifixion is 'the hour' toward which the entire Gospel has been moving. Every sign, every discourse, every 'I AM' statement finds its fulfillment here. John emphasizes Jesus' control and dignity throughout -- he carries his own cross, speaks from it with purpose, declares his work complete, and voluntarily surrenders his spirit. The cross is not a defeat but a coronation.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A perfect passive verb meaning 'it has been comple
wordStudies.meaning: A perfect passive verb meaning 'it has been completed, accomplished, fulfilled.' In commercial usage, it was stamped on receipts to mean 'paid in full.' Jesus declares that the work the Father gave him to do is fully accomplished. Nothing remains undone. The debt is paid, the mission is complete, the prophecies are fulfilled.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The branch used to lift a sponge of sour wine to J
wordStudies.meaning: The branch used to lift a sponge of sour wine to Jesus' lips. Hyssop was the same plant used to apply the Passover lamb's blood to the doorframes in Egypt (Exodus 12:22). John's detail connects Jesus' death to the original Passover -- the blood of the true Lamb is being applied.

verses.text: So Pilate then took Jesus, and flogged him.
verses.explainer: Then Pilate takes Jesus and has him flogged. Roman flogging (flagellum) used a leather whip embedded with bone or metal fragments that shredded the flesh. It was sometimes fatal on its own. Pilate may have hoped the flogging would satisfy the crowd's bloodlust without requiring crucifixion.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Roman flogging', 'explanation': 'The flagellum was a multi-thonged whip with pieces of bone, lead, or metal woven into the tips. The victim was stripped and tied to a post. The beating often exposed muscle and bone. Many prisoners died from flogging alone. Pilate likely intended it as a lesser punishment to avoid crucifixion.'}

verses.text: The soldiers twisted thorns into a crown, and put it on his head, and dressed him in a purple garment.
verses.explainer: The soldiers twist together a crown of thorns and put it on his head. They clothe him in a purple robe. The mockery is meant to ridicule his claim to kingship, but in John's ironic narrative, the soldiers are unwittingly telling the truth -- Jesus IS the king.

thenVsNow.then: Purple was the color of royalty, and crowns were symbols of sovereign authority. The soldiers create a parody of kingship: thorns instead of gold, a castoff robe instead of royal garments. They do not realize they are dressing the actual King of the universe.
thenVsNow.now: The image of the thorn-crowned Christ has become one of the most powerful symbols in world art and theology. True kingship, Jesus demonstrates, is defined not by power and luxury but by suffering and sacrifice.

verses.text: They kept saying, “Hail, King of the Jews!” and they kept slapping him.
verses.explainer: They keep coming up to him, saying: 'Hail, king of the Jews!' And they slap him in the face. Each slap accompanies a mock salute. The violence and ridicule continue from multiple soldiers.

verses.text: Then Pilate went out again, and said to them, “Behold, I bring him out to you, that you may know that I find no basis for a charge against him.”
verses.explainer: Once more Pilate comes out and says to the Jews gathered there: 'Look, I am bringing him out to you to let you know that I find no basis for a charge against him.' Pilate declares Jesus' innocence for the second time and presents the broken, flogged figure hoping the crowd will be satisfied.

verses.text: Jesus therefore came out, wearing the crown of thorns and the purple garment. Pilate said to them, “Behold, the man!”
verses.explainer: When Jesus comes out wearing the crown of thorns and the purple robe, Pilate says to them: 'Here is the man!' (Ecce homo in Latin.) Pilate means 'Look at this pitiful figure -- surely he is harmless.' But John means far more: behold the human being as God intended humanity to be -- the true Man, even in his suffering.

thenVsNow.then: Pilate's presentation of Jesus was a political maneuver -- showing a broken prisoner to demonstrate that he has been punished enough. The crowd was supposed to feel pity and drop their demand for execution.
thenVsNow.now: 'Ecce homo' has become one of the most iconic phrases in Western culture. It has inspired countless works of art. At its deepest level, it reveals what authentic humanity looks like: not power and self-preservation but self-giving love that absorbs violence without returning it.

verses.text:
When therefore the chief priests and the officers saw him, they shouted, saying, “Crucify! Crucify!”
Pilate said to them, “Take him yourselves, and crucify him, for I find no basis for a charge against him.”
verses.explainer: As soon as the chief priests and their officials see him, they shout: 'Crucify! Crucify!' Pilate says: 'You take him and crucify him. As for me, I find no basis for a charge against him.' The third declaration of innocence. Pilate tries to push the responsibility back onto the Jewish leaders.

verses.text: The Jews answered him, “We have a law, and by our law he ought to die, because he made himself the Son of God.”
verses.explainer: The Jewish leaders insist: 'We have a law, and according to that law he must die, because he claimed to be the Son of God.' The real charge finally emerges -- not political sedition but theological blasphemy. Jesus' claim to be the Son of God is what drives the death sentence.

verses.text: When therefore Pilate heard this saying, he was more afraid.
verses.explainer: When Pilate hears this, he is even more afraid. The claim to divine sonship unsettles the Roman governor. Roman culture was filled with stories of gods disguised as humans. What if this broken prisoner is more than he appears?

verses.text: He entered into the Praetorium again, and said to Jesus, “Where are you from?” But Jesus gave him no answer.
verses.explainer: He goes back inside the palace. 'Where do you come from?' he asks Jesus, but Jesus gives him no answer. The question is the right one -- 'Where do you come from?' is the central question of John's Gospel. But Pilate asks it too late and from the wrong motives.

verses.text: Pilate therefore said to him, “Aren’t you speaking to me? Don’t you know that I have power to release you, and have power to crucify you?”
verses.explainer: Pilate says: 'Do you refuse to speak to me? Don't you realize I have power either to free you or to crucify you?' Pilate asserts his authority. He holds the power of life and death.

verses.text:
Jesus answered,
“You would have no power at all against me, unless it were given to you from above. Therefore he who delivered me to you has greater sin.”
verses.explainer: Jesus answers: 'You would have no power over me if it were not given to you from above. Therefore the one who handed me over to you is guilty of a greater sin.' Jesus relativizes Pilate's power -- it is delegated from above. And he distinguishes degrees of guilt: Pilate is culpable, but the one who initiated the betrayal (Caiaphas or Judas) bears greater responsibility.

verses.text: At this, Pilate was seeking to release him, but the Jews cried out, saying, “If you release this man, you aren’t Caesar’s friend! Everyone who makes himself a king speaks against Caesar!”
verses.explainer: From then on, Pilate tries to set Jesus free, but the Jewish leaders keep shouting: 'If you let this man go, you are no friend of Caesar. Anyone who claims to be a king opposes Caesar.' The political threat is devastating. 'Friend of Caesar' (amicus Caesaris) was an official title of loyalty. To be accused of tolerating a rival king could end Pilate's career -- or his life.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Friend of Caesar', 'explanation': 'Amicus Caesaris was a formal designation indicating loyalty to the emperor. The Jewish leaders weaponize it -- suggesting that releasing Jesus would constitute disloyalty to Tiberius, who was notoriously paranoid about potential rivals. Pilate cannot afford the accusation.'}

verses.text: When Pilate therefore heard these words, he brought Jesus out, and sat down on the judgment seat at a place called “The Pavement”, but in Hebrew, “Gabbatha.”
verses.explainer: When Pilate hears this, he brings Jesus out and sits down on the judge's seat at a place known as the Stone Pavement (in Aramaic, Gabbatha). The Greek is ambiguous -- it could mean Pilate sat down on the judgment seat, or that he sat Jesus on the judgment seat. If the latter, it is one of John's deepest ironies: the prisoner on the judge's seat is the true Judge of all.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'The Stone Pavement (Gabbatha)', 'explanation': 'Archaeological evidence suggests this may be the large paved area discovered beneath the Convent of the Sisters of Zion near the Antonia Fortress. Scratched into the pavement stones are games Roman soldiers played, lending credibility to the mockery scene. The pavement served as both a public square and a judicial platform.'}

verses.text: Now it was the Preparation Day of the Passover, at about the sixth hour. He said to the Jews, “Behold, your King!”
verses.explainer: It is the day of Preparation of the Passover; it is about noon. 'Here is your king,' Pilate says to the Jews. John places the sentencing at noon on Preparation Day -- the exact hour when the Passover lambs were being slaughtered in the Temple. The Lamb of God is sentenced to death at the moment the symbolic lambs are being killed.

thenVsNow.then: The timing is theologically loaded. In the Temple courts, priests were beginning to slaughter the Passover lambs that would be eaten that evening. Jesus, the true Passover Lamb identified by John the Baptist in chapter 1, is condemned to death at precisely the same hour.
thenVsNow.now: The chronological precision reveals John's theological intentionality. Every detail of the passion narrative connects Jesus' death to the Passover sacrifice. The Lamb of God dies at the same time as the Passover lambs.

verses.text:
They cried out, “Away with him! Away with him! Crucify him!”
Pilate said to them, “Shall I crucify your King?”
The chief priests answered, “We have no king but Caesar!”
verses.explainer: But they shout: 'Take him away! Take him away! Crucify him!' 'Shall I crucify your king?' Pilate asks. 'We have no king but Caesar,' the chief priests answer. The chief priests renounce their messianic hope and their covenant with God in a single sentence. Israel's leaders, who should have acknowledged God as their only king, declare allegiance to Caesar.

verses.text: So then he delivered him to them to be crucified. So they took Jesus and led him away.
verses.explainer: Finally Pilate hands him over to them to be crucified. So the soldiers take charge of Jesus. Pilate capitulates. Political survival trumps justice. The innocent man is handed over because the governor lacks the courage to resist the crowd.

verses.text: He went out, bearing his cross, to the place called “The Place of a Skull”, which is called in Hebrew, “Golgotha”,
verses.explainer: Carrying his own cross, he goes out to the place of the Skull (which in Aramaic is called Golgotha). John does not mention Simon of Cyrene carrying the cross (as the Synoptics do). In John, Jesus carries his own cross -- sovereign and self-directed to the end.

verses.text: where they crucified him, and with him two others, on either side one, and Jesus in the middle.
verses.explainer: There they crucify him, and with him two others -- one on each side and Jesus in the middle. John narrates the crucifixion with stunning brevity -- just four words in Greek: 'they crucified him.' The restraint is deliberate. No description of the nails, the pain, the physical agony. The fact itself is enough.

verses.text: Pilate wrote a title also, and put it on the cross. There was written, “JESUS OF NAZARETH, THE KING OF THE JEWS.”
verses.explainer: Pilate has a notice prepared and fastened to the cross. It reads: JESUS OF NAZARETH, THE KING OF THE JEWS. The titulus (charge posted on the cross) serves legal and deterrent purposes. Pilate makes it a declaration of kingship that infuriates the Jewish leaders.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'The titulus (charge notice)', 'explanation': "Roman practice required posting the condemned person's crime on a wooden board (titulus) carried ahead of the prisoner or attached to the cross. Pilate's titulus does not state a crime but a title. Whether from spite, defiance, or unconscious prophecy, Pilate proclaims what the Gospel has been proclaiming: Jesus is the King."}

verses.text: Therefore many of the Jews read this title, for the place where Jesus was crucified was near the city; and it was written in Hebrew, in Latin, and in Greek.
verses.explainer: Many of the Jews read this sign, for the place where Jesus is crucified is near the city, and the sign is written in Aramaic, Latin and Greek. Three languages: the local vernacular, the language of law and empire, and the language of culture and philosophy. The kingship of Jesus is proclaimed to the entire known world.

verses.text: The chief priests of the Jews therefore said to Pilate, “Don’t write, ‘The King of the Jews,’ but, ‘he said, “I am King of the Jews.”’”
verses.explainer: The chief priests of the Jews protest to Pilate: 'Do not write The King of the Jews but that this man claimed to be king of the Jews.' They want the title changed from a statement of fact to a quote of a claim.

verses.text: Pilate answered, “What I have written, I have written.”
verses.explainer: Pilate answers: 'What I have written, I have written.' Pilate's refusal is one of his few moments of firmness. The title stands as written. The crucified carpenter is proclaimed king in the languages of the world, and no power on earth can revise it.

verses.text: Then the soldiers, when they had crucified Jesus, took his garments and made four parts, to every soldier a part; and also the coat. Now the coat was without seam, woven from the top throughout.
verses.explainer: When the soldiers crucify Jesus, they take his clothes, dividing them into four shares, one for each of them, with the undergarment remaining. This garment is seamless, woven in one piece from top to bottom. The seamless garment is a detail unique to John. Some scholars connect it to the high priest's robe, which was also woven in one piece -- Jesus dies as both king and priest.

verses.text:
Then they said to one another, “Let’s not tear it, but cast lots for it to decide whose it will be,” that the Scripture might be fulfilled, which says,
“They parted my garments among them.
For my cloak they cast lots.”
Therefore the soldiers did these things.
verses.explainer: 'Let's not tear it,' they say to one another. 'Let's decide by lots who will get it.' This happens that the scripture might be fulfilled that says: 'They divided my clothes among them and cast lots for my garment.' So this is what the soldiers do. Every detail of the crucifixion fulfills Scripture.

verses.text: But there were standing by the cross of Jesus his mother, and his mother’s sister, Mary the wife of Clopas, and Mary Magdalene.
verses.explainer: Near the cross of Jesus stand his mother, his mother's sister, Mary the wife of Clopas, and Mary Magdalene. Four women stand at the cross when most of the male disciples have fled. Their courage in remaining present at an execution is remarkable.

verses.text:
Therefore when Jesus saw his mother, and the disciple whom he loved standing there, he said to his mother,
“Woman, behold, your son!”
verses.explainer: When Jesus sees his mother there, and the disciple whom he loved standing nearby, he says to her: 'Woman, here is your son.' Even from the cross, Jesus attends to practical and relational responsibilities. He ensures his mother's care.

verses.text:
Then he said to the disciple,
“Behold, your mother!” From that hour, the disciple took her to his own home.
verses.explainer: And to the disciple: 'Here is your mother.' From that time on, this disciple takes her into his home. Jesus creates a new family at the cross. The beloved disciple and Mary are bound together not by blood but by Jesus' word. This models the new community formed at the foot of the cross.

thenVsNow.then: As the eldest son, Jesus was responsible for his mother's care. By entrusting her to the beloved disciple, he fulfills his family obligation even while dying. The act also symbolizes the new family of faith being created through the cross.
thenVsNow.now: This scene reveals that the church is meant to be a new family -- bound by shared faith in Jesus rather than by biological ties. The cross creates community.

verses.text:
After this, Jesus, seeing that all things were now finished, that the Scripture might be fulfilled, said,
“I am thirsty.”
verses.explainer: Later, knowing that everything has now been finished, and so that Scripture would be fulfilled, Jesus says: 'I am thirsty.' Even Jesus' thirst fulfills Scripture. The Creator of water experiences the deprivation of his own creation.

verses.text: Now a vessel full of vinegar was set there; so they put a sponge full of the vinegar on hyssop, and held it at his mouth.
verses.explainer: A jar of wine vinegar is there, so they soak a sponge in it, put the sponge on a stalk of the hyssop plant, and lift it to Jesus' lips. The hyssop detail is significant -- hyssop was used to apply the Passover lamb's blood to the doorframes in Egypt. The instrument of Passover redemption is now present at the death of the true Passover Lamb.

verses.text:
When Jesus therefore had received the vinegar, he said,
“It is finished.” He bowed his head, and gave up his spirit.
verses.explainer: When he has received the drink, Jesus says: 'It is finished.' With that, he bows his head and gives up his spirit. The Greek tetelestai is a word of triumph, not defeat. It means 'accomplished, completed, paid in full.' Jesus does not die as a victim but as a victor who has completed his mission. He does not have his life taken -- he gives up his spirit voluntarily.

thenVsNow.then: In commercial documents, tetelestai was written on receipts to indicate a debt fully paid. Jesus' final word declares that the debt of human sin has been paid in full. The work the Father gave him is complete -- nothing remains to be done.
